1 PROJECT DETAILS 
1.1 Summary Description of the Project 
The project, operated by Do ğu  S t a r  E l e k t r i k  Ü r e t i m  A.Ş., is the " İnegöl Integrated Solid Waste 
Storage and Disposal Facility" situated in The Republic of Türkiye, Bursa Province, İnegöl District, 
Yeniyörük Mahallesi, Körkuyu. It encompasses various components, namely the Mechanical 
separation facility, Refuse drive fuel (RDF) preparation facility, Biomethanization facility and 
power plant. These facilities are designed to in tegrate with the existing Solid Waste Landfill 
Facility, optimizing waste management practices. The project design got EIA approval on 27-
August-2021. 
By implementing advanced technologies and measures, the project aims to achieve significant 
greenhouse gas (GHG) emission reductions while generating electricity from biogas. The project 
will also import on-site produced RDF fuel to other power plants. 
The project site is strategically located adjacent  to the Solid Waste Landfill Facility of Bursa 
Metropolitan Municipality in Bursa Province, İnegöl District, Yeniyörük Mahallesi, Körkuyu. The 
proximity to the existing Solid Waste Landfill Facility allows for seamless integration and efficient 
waste management practices. The total area allocated for the project is 38,490.14 m
2.  
The project is expected to generate significant GHG emission reductions through its integrated 
waste management practices and energy producti on methods. By implementing the Mechanical 
separation facility, the Refuse drive fuel preparation facility, Biomethanization facility and the 
power plant, the project maximizes the utilization of municipal wastes and their conversion into 
valuable resources. Biogas, a byproduct of the biomethanization process, will be harnessed to 
produce electricity, thereby displacing ecolog ically unsound and clim ate unfriendly power 
generation sources. The project has an installed capacity of 8.484 MW
e and a licence capacity 
of 14.140 MW e. Additionally, the use of RDF further re duces reliance on fossil fuels, both 
imported and locally sourced. Ge nerated electricity is supplied to  Turkish national grid through 
İnegöl TM (No-154/ 34.5 kV) substation. 
Prior to the implementation of the project, municipal wastes from İnegöl, İznik, and Yeni şehir 
Districts were disposed of through regular storag e at the Solid Waste Landfill Facility. With the 
project implementation, organic fraction of muni cipal solid waste is mechanically separated and 
converted to biogas with digesters. As a result , Mechanical separation fa cility, Biomethanization 
facility and, Refuse drive fuel (RDF) preparatio n facility (implemented in 23-August 2023) were 
identified as key components of the project. Refu se drive fuel production selectively started with 
a part of combustible fraction of the MSW. The scenario lacked the integrated facility approach 
and the utilization of advanced technologies to effectively manage waste, produce renewable 
energy, and mitigate greenhouse gas emissions.  Furthermore, the absence of such measures

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
5 
resulted in direct emission of greenhouse gases into the atmosphere from the waste 
decomposition process, which the project seeks to address and reduce significantly. 
Over the projected 7-year credit ing period, the project is estimated to achieve a net emission 
reduction of 730,000 tCO 2e (metric tons of carbon dioxide equivalent) and 104,285 tCO 2e as 
annual average. The estimated net electricity generation for 7 years period is 349,547.209 MWh. 
This reduction is primarily attributed to the efficient biogas generation with reactors and 
electricity generation from generated biogas as a renewable energy source. These measures 
significantly contribute to reducing GHG emi ssions that would have been released through 
conventional waste decomposition processes.

1.2 Sectoral Scope and Project Type 
Sectoral scopes : 01 (Energy Industry – Renewable /Non-renewable Sources) and 13 (Waste 
handling and disposal)  
Methodology: ACM0022: Alternative waste treatment processes --- Version 3.0   
The project type is waste handling and disposal, and the project is not a grouped project. 
1.3 Project Eligibility 
The project is eligible under the scope of the VCS Program. Since the project meets the 
applicability conditions set out in the ACM0022. 
Project activity is a GHG emission avoidance project, following CDM Methodology ACM0022 
Alternative waste treatment processes--- Version 3.0. According to VCS standard v4.7: 
Section 2.1.1 states: “The scope of the VCS Program includes: 
1) The seven Kyoto Protocol greenhouse gases. 
2) Ozone-depleting substances (ODS). 
3) Project activities supported by a methodology approved under the VCS Program through 
the methodology development and review process.

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
6 
4) Project activities supported by a meth odology approved under an approved GHG 
program, unless explicitly excluded. 
5) Jurisdictional REDD+ programs and nested REDD+ projects as set out in the 
Jurisdictional and Nested REDD+ (JNR) Requirements.” 
Project activity of GHG destruction stays within VCS program where methane content of landfill 
gas is one of the “The seven Kyoto Protocol greenhouse gases” (item (1)), Project activity is also 
supported by methodology “ACM0022: Alternativ e waste treatment processes --- Version 3.0, 
developed by Clean Development Mechanism
1. Therefore, compliance with section 2.1.1 items 1 
and 4 enables project activity eligible. 
Section 2.1.2 states: “The scope of the VCS Prog ram excludes projects that can reasonably be 
assumed to have generated GHG emissions prim arily for the purpose of their subsequent 
reduction, removal, or destruction.” 
Project activity is an additional project activi ty that would not happen under business-as-usual 
scenario. Project is a solution to extend lifespan of İnegöl landfill and dispose organic fraction of 
mixed MSW in a more efficient way. Bursa province is part of East Marmara region 2 (statistically 
and economically) and no other province employ similar biomethanization facility. Refuse drive 
fuel production from MSW (fuel preparation facility from MSW only, no incineration on-site) is also 
an additional activity, since majority of disposal sites are focused on inorganic material recovery 
(such as metals and plastics)
3. 
Section 2.1.3 states: “The VCS Program also excludes the following project activities under the 
circumstances indicated in Table 1, below.”

The project start date is 31-December-2020, whic h corresponds with the issuance of the partial 
acceptance7 date for the first gas engine. This milestone marked the commencement of 
electricity generation from biogas and supply to the national grid. Project is listed at verra registry 
site on 23-March-2023. According to VCS standard v4.7 section 3.8.1, "Non-AFOLU projects shall 
complete validation within two years of the project start date". Project activity started on 31-
December-2020 and got an exemption on 24-November-2022 and 23-June-2023. Exemption 
given on 23-June-2023 has allowed the project to  complete request for validation before 31-
December-2023. An opening made with the vali dation body has been completed on 25-May-
2023. 
1.9 Project Crediting Period 
The project activity adopts a renewable crediting period of 7 years which can be renewed for 
maximum 2 times. 
According to Verra standard v4.7 section 3.8, "T he project start date of a non-AFOLU project is 
the date on which the project began generating GHG emission reductions or carbon dioxide 
removals". Project start date is the date of provisional acceptance date of first gas engine where 
electricity generation from respective gas engine is allowed. Continuous electricity generation on-
site started on June-2021. The project uses an aerobic digestors (PFR type, 21 days retention 
time of fresh waste) to utilize biogas only. The flare equipment is part of the booster unit and 
designed to be used during emergency situations. The flare was installed on-site and tested by 
the manufacturer on-site before the commission ing of gas engines. Between equipment tests 
and gas engine, flare equipment was not operational.  
1st Crediting period: 31-December-2020 – 30-December-2027

1.11 Description of the Project Activity 
Under project activity, fractions of fresh waste that is bound to be landfilled are mechanically 
separated and utilized for multiple purposes. Or ganic fractions (food wastes and park & yard 
trimmings) of municipal solid waste (MSW) are mechanically separated and sent to digesters 
where generated biogas is utilized for electricity production. Half of liquid digestate is recycled 
back, and the other half is sent to leachate treatment center. Solid digestate is sent to landfill 
since it’s considered unfit to be used as fertilizer by legislations (due to presence of inert 
materials such as glass and plastics that cannot be mechanically separated). Generated 
electricity is supplied to Turkish national grid through İnegöl TM (No-154/ 34.5 kV) substation.

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
13 
Starting from 23-August 2023, Refuse Drive Fuel (RDF) production implemented in the Integrated 
Solid Waste Management Plant. 27 tons/h RDF production capacity is the maximum capacity. 
Availability of material will determine the need for capacity increase. 
Mixed MSW collected from the residential areas carried by specialized garbage trucks where 
MSW is contained, and leakage is prevented. Ther e are two types of garbage trucks used for the 
transportation of MSW; trucks with a size of 27 ton carrying capacity and trucks with a size of 7 
ton carrying capacity. All trucks are recorded with a digital system where each truck is identified 
by a magnetic band stuck to truck. Weight of full and empty truck are recorded with exact time 
and this data is kept by the plant. All trucks dump their load into the waste receiving unit of the 
facility (waterproof concrete diagonal open pit) and a crane transfer accepted MSW to Mechanical 
Separation Facility. MSW is separated into biodegradable waste, residual waste (directly sent to 
landfill) waste suitable for recycling. After the introduction of RDF, combustible waste will be 
collected separately and utilized for RDF production. Facility has automated machine that does 
majority of the job while manual labor is used fo r more delicate jobs such as sorting collected 
plastic waste. 
In the mechanical separation plant, 2 lines with a capacity of 40 tons/hour each were operational 
in 2021. In 2040, the Facility is expected to op erate 3 shifts for 17 hours, which is the plant’s 
maximum capacity. 
For the base year 2021, Waste characterization is  approximately; 57.34% biodegradable waste, 
14.97% recyclable waste, 9.49% residual waste, and 18.18% is composed of combustible waste. 
In addition, it is assumed that 0.02% of hazardous waste is in mixed municipal waste, and this 
value is excluded from the calculation. For the target year 2040, approximately 66,54% of the 
total waste that will enter the Mechanical Separation Facility is biodegradable waste, 1,32% is 
recyclable waste, 11.01% is residual waste and 21% is combustible waste. In addition, 0.03% of 
hazardous waste is estimated to be mixed with municipal waste, and this value is excluded from 
the calculation. 
In the mechanical sorting facility, mixed municipa l wastes accepted to the waste reception area 
is first transported by overhead crane and fed to the bag opener. The material coming out of the 
bag opener enters the first drum (Trommel/Drum) sieve through the conveyor. In the first drum 
sieve, the material is separated automatically in terms of dimensions. These dimensions are 
under 160 mm, between 160-300 mm and 300 mm (oversize) in the first drum sieve. The second 
drum is sent to the sieve to separate the pr edominantly biodegradable fraction in the 160 mm 
size material. In the second drum sieve, the material is separated into two as below 80 mm and 
above 80 mm. The material coming out of the second drum sieve in the range of 80-160 mm and 
the material in the range of 16 0-300 mm coming out of the first drum sieve enters a ballistic 
separator separately. Ballistic separator material  is defined as 2D non-voluminous material 
(Paper, cardboard, nylon, film, etc.), 3D bulky material (Plastics, metals, etc.) and 50 mm material 
under sieve. divided into three fractions. Due to the nature of the 2D material waste 
characterization mentioned here, RDF is a material with highly flammable property (calorific 
value), easy to physically process (break in to smaller sizes). Thus, RDF material will be

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
14 
automatically separated. Combustible wastes sepa rated in the Mechanical Separation Facility 
will be directed to the RDF Facility via belts. Biodegradable waste, which is expressed as under-
sieve material, is fed to the Biomethanization Faci lity via belts. In addi tion, digestates will be 
directed at a, are collected in th e closed trailer that is located ju st below the belts and is sent to 
the predesignated landfill lot.

Combustible non-hazardous wastes (wood and woody products, normally directly sent to landfill) 
that are generated within Bursa also can be accepted to the RDF Preparation Facility if sufficient 
amount can be collected regularly. It’s estima ted that 50% of inlet MSW can be used for RDF 
production where 20% will be discarded to landfi ll as inert material and remaining 80% will be 
RDF with a 2500 kcal/kg calorific value.  
The list of units to be included in the RDF Prepar ation Facility to be established within the scope 
of the project is given below. 
 Crusher (Pre-Chuck) 
 RDF Collection Area (to be used jointly with Mechanical Separation Plant)

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
16 
The separated biodegradable fraction of MSW is di rectly fed into the Biomethanization Facility 
unit via the belt. Biodegradable wastes and residual  wastes that are sent to sanitary landfill are 
kept in a closed trailer during collection up to a point where they  are disposed to the landfill.  
The biodegradable wastes coming from the Mechani cal Separation Facility are taken directly to 
the Biomethanization Facility waste acceptance un it via the belt. Biodegradable wastes are taken 
from the waste acceptance unit to the bunker (with agitator) and fed to the fermentation reactors 
after the desired homogeneity of the wastes is achieved. 
Biodegradable wastes coming to the Biomethanization Facility will come to a completely closed 
waste reception unit. This area has a total size of  16 m x 12 m x 3.1 m and a total volume of 595 
m
3 and is large enough to collect waste for at least 1 day. 
A total of 4 reactors with a maximum capacity of 180 tons/day (3.512 m 3/reactor) are built in 
the Biomethanization Facility.  
The units to be found in the system are given below. 
 Anaerobic Reactors 
 Mixing System 
 Gas Storage Unit 
 Gas Purification System 
 Gas Engines 
 Booster Unit (with Flare)

The biogas generated in the Biomethanization Facility and the methane gas that will be drawn 
from the Landfill Gas collection system from the Landfill Area will be converted into electricity 
and heat in the Electricity Generation Facility to be  established. All of the electrical energy to be 
obtained will be transferred to the interconnect ed system and economic gain will be achieved. 
The waste heat is used in the Biomethanization Plant for energy efficiency where heat demand 
is higher due to thermophilic conditions.. 
The licensed capacity of the electricity generation facility is 14.140 MW
e, corresponding to ten 
gas engines, each with a rated capacity of 1.414 MW e. However, as of 24-May-2023, only six gas 
engines (totaling 8.484 MW e) have been installed and are operational. This decision was based 
on the actual waste intake volumes and biogas  production potential, both of which are 
constrained by municipal solid waste (MSW) deliveries regulated by Bursa Municipality. Given 
these constraints and the phased nature of the investment, the remaining four engines under 
the license have not been installed and are not expected to be realized within the project lifetime. 
The project’s feasibility analysis was built upon the 9.898 MW
e installed capacity (seven engines), 
with a net electricity generation estimate of 59,455 MWh/year. According to cumulative 
weighbridge report of project proponent , the project site received 262,970.370 tons of MSW in 
2022, which equates to approximately 720.46 tons per day. Assuming that 45%
8 of this incoming 
MSW is suitable for biomethanization, around 324.2 tons/day can be processed in anaerobic 
digesters. Based on a standard yield of 130 m³ of biogas per ton of organic waste
9, this translates 
to 42,146 m³ of biogas per day. Wi th an assumed methane content of 56% 10, the daily methane 
production is estimated at 23,601.76 m³. Using a lower heating value (LHV) of 9.97 kWh/m³ 11 
for methane and assuming 41% electrical efficiency of the gas engines 12, this results in 
96,476.91 kWh of gross electricity per day (equivalent to approximately 4.02 MWh/h).

Under the highest waste intake conditions (1,500 tons/day mixed MSW, 45% is suitable for 
biomethanization facility. It corresponds to  675 ton/day of organics fed to anaerobic 
digesters.)13, daily methane generation would reach 49,140 m³, enabling a gross theoretical 
output of approximately 8.369 MWh. However, with an 8000 operational hours per year 14 85.1% 
efficiency 15 and internal consumption adjustment 16, the maximum net electricity generation is 
calculated as 51,264.856 MWh/year. This figure  is consistent with feasibility estimates and 
reflects real-world operational constraints and biogas variability. Therefore, despite the legal 
license allowing 14.140 MW
e capacity, the project is designed and operated for a maximum 
generation of 9.898 MW e. Based on current waste availability and realistic technical 
performance, the project will not reach licence capacity.

1.17 Sustainable Development Contributions 
The Project will contribute to sustainabl e development in the following ways:  
 Reduce CH4 emission. The project will recover and destroy biogas that consists mainly of 
greenhouse gas methane and would otherwise be released directly into the atmosphere, 
effectively reducing greenhouse gas emissi ons, which contributes to the Turkey’s 
commitment to carbon dioxide emissions;  
 Increase electricity production from renewable sources, which will certainly reduce the 
consumption of fossil fuel in Turkish Power Grid and further help to achieve the national 
action to promote renewable energy development;  
 Improve the operational safety of the landfills. The project reduces the potential safety 
hazards of landfill gas explosion by recycling landfill gas and improves the safety of 
landfill operation;  
 The project will also provide new jobs opport unities and increase tax revenue, which will 
have a positive effect on the local economy.  
 Recycled material from MSW is utilized for material recycling and energy generation via 
biomethanization, decrease landfilled MSW amount thus contribute to sustainability 
 Nevertheless, it is still possible to note that  the project will make positive contributions 
to five Sustainable Developmen t Goals (SDGs). These are:  
SDG Goal 5: Gender equality: Target 5.5, Ensure women’s full and effective participation and 
equal opportunities for leadership at all levels of decision making in political, economic and 
public life.  Sub indicator 5.5.2 description is “Proportion of women in managerial positions”. 
There is an initiative called “30 Percent Club” to promote women participation in the boards and 
top executive teams internationally and domestically. Türkiye’s 30 Percent Club initiative’s aims 
are women participation in the boards and top executive teams of BIST Star Market, Main Market

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
29 
companies and BIST 100 companies 20. Biotrend company has joined the initiative 21 and number 
of women managers on board satisfy 30% condition 2223. KPIs (5.5.1) to track the projects’ 
contribution to SDG 5 is number of women in managerial positions to total number of managerial 
positions. There is no appropriate value is found for this SDG indicator in Türkiye, and voluntary 
contribution to 30 percent club and satisfying the condition should satisfy the KPI 5.5.1. 
Biotrend's number of women managers on top board is 3 out of 9 positions.  
SDG Goal 7: Affordable and Clean Energy : Target 7.2: Increase global percentage of renewable 
energy. Sub indicator 7.2.1 description is “Renew able energy share in the total final energy 
consumption”. The proposed Project is a waste to power project that will generate renewable 
energy by capturing methane from municipal waste and utilizing it to produce thermal and electric 
energy through gas engine systems. By supplying renewable energy generated at the plant to the 
national grid, the proposed Project will contribute to increasing the share of renewable energy in 
the global energy mix and the proportion of the population with primary reliance on clean fuels 
and technology. KPIs (7.2.1) to track the projects’ contribution to SDG 7 is amount of renewable 
energy supplied to grid for consumption. It was estimated that the project will generate 
349,547.209 MWh(gross production) over the first crediting period.  
SDG Goal 8: Decent Work and Economic Gr owth: T arget 8.5: Full employment and decent work 
with equal pay and Target 8.8: Protect labor rights and promote safe working environment. Sub 
indicator 8.5.2 description is “Unemployment rate, by sex, age and persons with disabilities “ and 
sub indicator 8.8.1 is “Fatal and non-fatal occupa tional injuries per 100,000 workers, by sex and 
migrant status“. The demand for food and electric energy is rapidly increasing in Turkey for 
various reasons, such as industrialization, urbanization, economic development, and population 
growth. The country's external dependence on ag ricultural products has intensified because the 
increasing demand cannot be met by a decreasing  domestic agricultural production capacity 
which is due to several reasons, such as shrinkag e of agricultural land, increasing migration to 
urban spaces from rural areas where agricultural production is densely located, and dramatic 
increase in the cost of inputs for agricultural production. Therewithal, Turkey, which cannot meet 
its increasing electricity demand due to the de privation of conventional resources used to 
generate energy, such as coal, oil, and natura l gas, has also become foreign-dependent on 
energy. Through its implementation, the proposed project activity will contribute to reducing 
Turkey's foreign dependency by generating renewable energy out of municipal wastes. In addition, 
as a bio-based renewable energy technology impl ementation, the proposed project activity will

1.18 Additional Information Relevant to the Project  
Leakage Management 
Leakage emissions are considered  as per the applicable tool. 
Leakage emissions are consider ed as per TOOL14: Project and leakage emissions from 
anaerobic digesters. Default value is adopted for project emissions from biogas leak and aerobic 
digestate management. 
Under project activity, generated biogas goes di rectly to gas engines where flare system can 
destroy excess gas during emergency situations. There are multiple pressure sensors with 
digester and at the booster that can detect leaks based on unusual pressure drops. When such 
conditions occur, leak location is found by plant technicians and bypass lines continue operation. 
HDPE pipes allow leak repairs an d gas balloon lets extra down-tim e for repairs. Under worst case 
where such measures cannot be applied for gene rated biogas, safety valves can be used for 
venting extra biogas and electricity production wou ld be halted. No such scenario realized before 
and plant operation is constantly monitored for maximum performance and leak prevention.

No commercially sensitive information has been ex cluded from the public version of the project 
description.

2.1 No Net Harm 
The Ministry of Environment and Urbanization has authorized the Environmental Impact 
Assessment (EIA) Report. According to Turkish ru les and regulations, an EIA's permission may 
only be granted if the project in question has no detrimental effects on the environment or the 
economy, thus the project has no net harm. All kinds of preventive measures will be taken to 
minimize the odor, dust, leachate, gas, and simi lar negative effects that may arise from the 
facility. These preventive measures are explai ned in the Environmental Impact Assessment 
Report in further detail. The landfill floor will be  constructed in accordance with the provisions of 
the "Regulation on the Landfilling of Wastes" and the Circular on the Preparation of the Sanitary 
Landfills Implementation Project, numbered 20 18/15, which was published in the Official 
Gazette dated 26-March-2010 and numbered 27533. A letter of conformity will be obtained from 
the relevant institution regarding the impermeability of the sealed floors and the impermeable 
pools throughout the project. If it is determined  that the springs, fountains, groundwaters and 
boreholes in the vicinity of the activity area are adversely affected in terms of water quantity and 
quality, the activity will be stopped immediat ely, and the project will be terminated, and any 
damage will be borne by  the activity owner.

Grievance Handling Procedure 
Steps Taken Once a Grievance is Received 
Grievances are managed through a structured five-step process. First, grievances are 
registered, with external stakeholders submitting them via email, website, social media, phone, 
meetings, or physical forms at facility loca tions, while internal stakeholders use the İK.FR.012 
Internal Stakeholder Form, which can be submitted via email or designated complaint boxes at 
the facility. All grievances are formal ly recorded using tracking forms (KU İ.FR.002 for external 
and İK.FR.013 for internal complaints). 
Second, grievances are classified into three le vels: Level 1 for one-time or minor incidents, 
Level 2 for recurring complaints such as environm ental impacts, and Level 3 for legal violations 
or serious stakeholder concerns requiring urge nt intervention. The Corporate Communication 
Department handles external complaints, while the Human Resources Department manages 
internal grievances, ensuring appropriate classification. 
Third, grievances are assigned to the relevant  department or facility manager, with the 
Corporate Communication or HR Department ensuring that responsible units address 
complaints within a seven-day window. Fourth, the responsible department investigates and 
proposes corrective actions. For severe ca ses classified as Level 3 grievances, top

management is involved to allocate necessary re sources, and resolution is expected within 14 
working days unless further investigation is required. 
Finally, responses and closures are manage d by informing the complainant about the 
resolution. If the complaint was anonymous, responses are communicated through public 
channels such as announcements, social media, or official statements. Additionally, corrective 
actions are documented to prevent recurrence. 
Expected Timeline for Resolution 
The grievance resolution process follows a clear timeline. Initial acknowledgment occurs within 
seven working days after receipt, and an investig ation with an action plan is developed within 
three working days of classification. The resoluti on is expected within 14 working days unless 
further action from senior management is required. Once resolved, the complainant is notified 
through their preferred communication channel,  while anonymous reports are addressed via 
public announcements. 
Measures to Ensure Impartiality in Grievance Handling 
To ensure impartiality in grievance handling, several measures are in place. Neutrality and 
confidentiality are maintained by allowing grievances to be submitted anonymously, with no 
personal identification required, and employees are protected from retaliation under company 
policy. The independent review process involves the Internal Audit Department, which, if 
necessary, conducts confidential interviews and investigations, with senior management 
overseeing Level 3 complaints to ensure transparency. 
Clear categorization and accountability are ma intained by having different teams handle 
external and internal complaints to avoid conflicts of interest. Complaints are categorized 
objectively based on severity, and resolutions are tracked. Furthermore, public reporting and 
documentation ensure that all grievances are logged and stored to maintain an audit trail. If a 
complaint is recurring, additional mitigation measures are implemented to prevent future 
occurrences. 
2.3 Environmental Impact 
An environmental impact assessment dated in 10 -September-2020 was prepared to assess the 
environmental effects of the project activity. The report has been prepared in accordance with 
Turkey’s national standards. Therefore, the project is implemented according to the national laws 
and regulations if the environmental precautions st ated in the report are applied. EIA has been 
conducted to assess all potential impacts and minimize through measures adopted. 
A public participation meeting was held on 12-December-2019, within the scope of the 
Environmental Impact Assessment Regulation, whi ch was published in the Official Gazette dated 
25-November-2014 and numbered 29186. The meeting’s purpose was to inform the public about 
the investment and to receive their opinio ns and suggestions regarding the project.

meeting was initiated by the meeting chairman, Bursa Environment and Urbanization EIA and 
Environmental Permits Branch Manager Ahmet PARLAK, making an opening speech. During the 
meeting, the presentation made by the EIA Report Expert regarding the features and 
environmental effects of the project was completed, and the public's opinions and suggestions 
were received after the presentation. 
It was stated by Hayrettin DEMİRTAŞ, a resident of Yeniyörük village, that there was a dog problem 
caused by the sanitary landfill; In this context, it was stated that the facility was surrounded by 
wire fences in order to prevent unco ntrolled entrances into the facility. 
Yeniyörük village resident Ramazan ATE Ş asked how far the nearest settlement should be to the 
project site within the scope of the "Regulation on Regular Landfilling of  Wastes". Within the 
scope of the Communique on Management of the facili ty, it is stated that the closest distance of 
the facility border to the residential areas sh ould be 250 meters. The closest settlement within 
the scope of the project is Yeniyörük District, located at a distance of 735 meters. 
Cemil DOLDUR, a resident of Yeniyörük village, asked about the wastewater spilling on the roads 
and what precautions should be taken regarding the wastewater, and it was stated that in this 
context, drainage systems would be created to prevent the leachate from mixing with the soil, 
underground water systems and surface waters, and the impact on the environment would be 
prevented. In addition, the leachate collected by the drainage system in the existing solid waste 
landfill is transferred to the le achate balancing pool. The leacha te collected in the leachate 
balancing pond is currently given to the Wastewater Treatment Plant of the Bursa Water and 
Sewerage Administration General Directorate. 
The meeting was ended smoothly by Bursa Enviro nment and Urban Planning Directorate and the 
meeting was recorded with the minutes. 
The project was open for public comment from 23-March-2023 to 22-April-2023.

3.3 Project Boundary 
According to the ACM0022, the spatial extent of the project boundary is the SWDS where the 
waste is disposed of in the baseline, anaerobic lagoons or sludge pits treating organic wastewater 
in the baseline, and the site of the alternative waste treatment process(es). The boundary also 
includes on-site electricity and/or heat generation and use, on-site fuel use and the wastewater 
treatment plant used to treat the wastewater by-products of the alternative waste treatment 
process(es). The project boundary does not include facilities for waste collection and transport.  
Management of the İnegöl landfill and its related landfill gas (LFG) activities are not included in 
the project activities. The project focuses on waste diversion from landfills by utilizing mechanical 
separation, anaerobic digestion, and RDF production to minimize landfilling. Only residual 
organic fractions that cannot be processed furthe r are sent to landfill. However, this disposal 
occurs within the designated sludge area of the la ndfill, where it is used as landfill cover rather 
than conventional waste disposal. 
In the baseline scenario, all municipal solid waste (MSW) would have been landfilled, leading to 
methane emissions from anaerobic decomposition. In the project scenario, the waste undergoes 
mechanical separation, anaerobic digestion, and RDF conversion (for inorganic combustibles) 
before final disposal, significantly reducing la ndfill emissions. The methane avoidance due to 
waste treatment and energy generation is incorpor ated into the emission reduction calculations. 
The portion of organic waste used as landfill cover undergoes minimal anaerobic decomposition 
compared to traditional MSW disposal. Thus , methane emissions from this portion are 
significantly lower than the baseline. 
Therefore, mechanically separated organic fracti on of MSW and combustible materials that can 
be converted into Refuse Drive Fuel are considered as material inputs and outputs are also 
tracked to the project boundary limit. Organic fraction of MSW is decomposed into biogas, which 
is utilized for electricity generation, solid digestat e is sent to landfill to designated sludge area 
first then used as cover material since it’s found improper to be used as fertilizer and liquid 
digestate is partially recycled and remaining fraction goes to wastewater treatment facility. 
Combustible waste types such as textiles, some plastics and diapers are converted into RDF and 
used for electricity generation in Cine Biomass Power Plant and Ezine Biomass Power Plant which 
are operated by subsidiary company of İzmir Doğu Star Elektrik Üretim A.Ş. Management of İnegöl 
landfill and related LFG activities  are not under project activity.

3.4 Baseline Scenario 
According to paragraph 15 of the applied methodology ACM0022: Alternative waste treatment 
processes --- Version 3.0, ‘Identify the baseline  scenario and demonstrate additionality using 
“TOOL02: Combined tool to identify the baseline scenario and demonstrate additionality”.’ Must 
be followed.  
The baseline scenario and additionality are investigated as per “TOOL02: Combined tool to 
identify the baseline scenario and demonstrat e additionality” within  section 3.4 and 3.5. 
According to paragraph 16 of the applied meth odology ACM0022: Alternative waste treatment 
processes --- Version 3.0, ‘The following alternativ es or combinations of these alternatives shall, 
inter alia, be considered for the baseline scenario of the fresh waste treatment: 
(a) M1: The project activity without being registered as a CDM project activity (i.e. any 
(combination) of the waste treatment processes listed in Table 2); 
(b) M2: Disposal of the fresh waste in a SWDS with a partial capture of the LFG and flaring 
of the captured LFG;

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
55 
(f) W6: Using the wastewater for co-composting (the project activity implemented without being 
registered as a CDM project activity);  
(g) W7: Other treatment processes provided in ta ble 6.3, Volume 5, chapter 6 of the IPCC 2006 
guidelines for greenhouse gas inventory. ‘ 
Treatment of the wastewater is not included in the project activity, thus none of the options are 
applicable for baseline scenarios. 
According to paragraph 18 of the applied methodology ACM0022: Alternative waste treatment 
processes --- Version 3.0, ‘For electricity generation  the following alternatives or combinations of 
these alternatives, inter alia, shall be considered: 
(a) P1: Electricity generated as an output of one of the waste treatment processes listed in 
Table 1, not undertaken as a CDM project activity; 
(b) P2: Use of an existing or construction of a new on-site or off-site fossil fuel fired 
cogeneration plant; 
(c) P3: Existing or new construction of an on-site or off-site renewable based cogeneration 
plant; 
(d) P4: Existing or new construction of an on-sit e or off-site fossil fuel fired electricity plant; 
(e) P5: Existing or new construction of an on-site or off-site renewable based electricity plant; 
(f) P6: Electricity generation in existing an d/or new grid-connected  electricity plants.’ 
Before the project activity, the project area was already being used landfill 42 and converted to a 
sanitary landfill. The landfill site had a power plant commissioned on 30-October-2018 (2.4 MWe 
licence capacity). Therefore, P6 is a plausible scenario for project activity. Greenfield facility is 
connected to the national grid. 
According to paragraph 19 of the applied methodology ACM0022: Alternative waste treatment 
processes --- Version 3.0, ‘For heat generation, one or a combination of the following alternatives, 
inter alia, shall be considered: 
(a) H1: Heat generated as a by-product from one of the processes for waste treatment listed in 
Table 1, not undertaken as a CDM project activity; 
(b) H2: Use of (an) existing or construction of (a) new on-site or off-site fossil fuel fired 
cogeneration plant(s);5 
(c) H3: Use of (an) existing or construction of (a) new on-site or off-site renewable based 
cogeneration plant(s);6

or air heater(s);

boiler(s) or air heater(s); 
(f) H6: District heat; 
(g) H7: Other heat generation technologies (e.g. heat pumps or solar energy).’ 
Treatment of the heat generation is not included in the project activity, thus none of the options 
are applicable for baseline scenarios. 
According to paragraph 20 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “Project participants shall 
demonstrate that the identified baseline fuel used for generation of heat is available in 
abundance in the host country and there is no supply constraint. In case of supply constraints 
(e.g. partial or seasonal supply), project participants shall consider the period when there are 
availability constraints and choose the fuel(s) that result in the most conservative estimation of 
baseline emissions.”. Since project activity does not include heat generation, this condition is not 
applicable.  
According to paragraph 21 of the applied method ology ACM0022, “Detailed justifications shall 
be provided and documented in the CDM-PDD for the selected baseline fuel. As a conservative 
approach, the lowest carbon-intensive fuel, such  as natural gas, may be used throughout all 
periods of the year.”. Since project activity does not include heat generation, this condition is not 
applicable.  
According to paragraph 22 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “For the supply of upgraded 
biogas to a natural gas distribution network, the baseline is assumed to be the continued supply 
with natural gas.”. Since project activity does not include supply of upgraded biogas to a natural 
gas distribution network, this condition is not applicable. 
According to paragraph 23 of the applied method ology ACM0022, “In applying Sub-step 1b of 
the tool, mandatory applicable legal and regulatory requirements may include mandatory LFG 
capture or destruction requirements because of sa fety issues or local environmental regulations. 
Other policies could include local policies promotin g productive use of LFG, such as those for the 
production of renewable energy, or those that prom ote the processing of fresh waste.”. In Türkiye 
there are no laws and regulations that require landfills to flare or utilize generated landfill gas. 
According to paragraph 24 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “If the products or by-products 
are used by another CDM project activity and if the project participants of both CDM projects are 
the same, then the following provision shall be followed: 
 
(a) The by-products shall be considered to have market value when assessing additionality of the 
project activity applying this methodology;

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
57 
(b) The by-products shall be considered to have  no value when assessing additionality of the 
second project activity.” 
 
Under project activity, refuse drive fuel is produced from MSW and exported to off-site. For the 
additionality analysis, RDF market value is considered. 
 
According to paragraph 25 of the applied me thodology ACM0022, “Project activities 
implementing greenfield composting facilities for the treatment of MSW are deemed 
automatically additional when any of conditions be low is fulfilled: (a) The host country is a LDC; 
or  
(b) MSW collection coverage is below 50 per cent  for the applicable geographical region; or  
(c) MSW collection coverage is 50–80 per cent fo r the applicable geographical region and the 
waste received by the project composting facility  does not have formal segregation of wet and 
dry waste (i.e. excluding recycling by the informal sector); or  
(d) No gate/tipping fees are received by the pr oject composting facility or any upstream waste 
collection or segregation facility which is cont ractually bound to provide waste to the project 
composting facility; or  
(e) Less than two per cent of the collected MSW of  the municipality (or the region from where the 
MSW treated by the project activity is sourced) are treated by composting in the year before the 
PDD is published for global stakeholder consultation.”  
Since project activity does not include composting or co-composting, this condition is not 
applicable. 
According to paragraph 26 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “For project activities that are 
deemed automatically additional above, the base line scenario is assumed to be the continued 
disposal of the fresh waste in the nearest SWDS  (where the LFG is released or captured and 
destructed through flaring to comply with regu lations or contractual re quirements, to address 
safety and odour concerns, or for other reasons). If condition (d) or (e) applies, it shall also be 
demonstrated that 80 per cent of the collected MSW of the municipality (or the region from where 
the MSW treated by the project activity is sourced) are treated in SWDS or stockpiles or are 
dumped in the year before the PDD is publis hed for global stakeholder consultation.”  
Project activities that are associated with the methodology ACM0022 are not automatically 
additional. Therefore, this condition is not applicable. 
According to paragraph 27 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “The collection coverage of 
MSW may be estimated as: (1) the quantity of  waste collected divided by the total waste 
generation, or (2) the population covered by waste collection service divided by the total 
population. The quantity of waste collected may be (1) obtained from the municipal waste 
authority, (2) based on local statistics, or (3) based on the MSW accepted by all waste processing

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
58 
facilities, including open dump sites. Total waste generation may be calculated using data on 
population and/or waste generation per capita.”. 
This condition is applicable. Colle ction coverage of MSW is not used since project activity already 
set its capacity based on practice of Bursa municipality, which is responsible for waste 
management of whole Bursa province. The Municipa lity collects MSW from all districts of the 
province and treats fresh waste in proper existing  facilities. The quantity of collected waste and 
all inlet and outlet materials are measure through the Weighbridge of İnegöl solid waste 
management facility, which is co ntrolled by project proponent.  
3.5 Additionality 
In the case of the proposed project activity, the Methodology of ACM0022: Alternative waste 
treatment processes, version 3.0 43 is applied to the proposed project activity. Additionality for 
the project activity is demonstrated using Tool 02 Combined tool to identify the baseline scenario 
and demonstrate addition ality (Version 07.0)44.  
According to TOOL02, the project partic ipants shall follow the four steps:

STEP 2. Barrier analysis; 
STEP 3. Investment analysis; 
STEP 4. Common practice analysis. 
STEP 0. Demonstration that a proposed project activity is the first-of-its-kind 
The project is not the first-of-its-kind project

of electricity, the baseline scenario will be identified based on these two parts (heat generation 
is excluded). In identifying baseline alternatives for the treatment of the fresh waste and 
electricity generation, the following alternatives  or combinations of these alternatives are 
considered:

Outcome of Step 1a: List of plausible alte rnative scenarios to the project activity 
There is no national regulation which would require the recovery or flaring of the LFG emitted 
from landfill sites. The relevant environmental legislation does not force municipalities to 
effectively control LFG emissions from landfills; henc e, it is rather a tender for the rights to use 
the site for power generation purposes. The LFG would be partially captured and flared. Thus, 
baseline scenario M1, M2  and M3 are applicable. 
P6 is a plausible scenario for the project activity. There was no renewable based electricity plant 
on-site before the project activity. 
Step 1b: Consistency with mandator y applicable laws and regulations 
All the credible alternatives identified above co mply with Turkey's current laws and regulatory 
requirements. The project activity is not the only  alternative scenario that follows all mandatory 
regulations with which there is general compliance. 
Step 2: Barrier Analysis 
There are no technology barriers, acceptability barriers nor financial barriers that may prevent 
these two alternative scenarios from occurring. As per Tool 02 Combined tool to identify the 
baseline scenario and demonstrate additionality (Version 07.0), go to Step 3 investment analysis. 
STEP 3 – Investment analysis;  
The investment analysis has been done in order to make an economic and financial evaluation 
of the project. No public funding or ODA are available in Turkey for finance of this type of projects. 
For investment analysis, loan conditions have been determined considering the average market 
rates/term sheets signed with the banks. Investment analysis is carried as per the 
methodological Tool 2: “Combined tool to identify the baseline scenario and demonstrate 
additionality”, Version 07.0.  
Sub-step 2a - Determine appropriate analysis method

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
65 
There are three options for the determin ation of analysis method which are: 
 Simple Cost Analysis 
 Investment Comparison Analysis and 
 Benchmark Analysis 
Since Project generates economic benefits from sales of electricity, the simple cost analysis is 
not applicable. Also, since the baseline of the project is generation of electricity by the grid, no 
alternative investment is considered at issue. So , it has been decided to use benchmark analysis 
for evaluation of the project investment. 
Sub-step 2b: Option III. Apply benchmark analysis 
For benchmark analysis, figure defined by World Bank for similar project types have been used 
which has been given as 20% (pre-tax) 49 for IRR by a report generated in June 2017. For the 
proposed project, in order to reach this IRR values, average electricity tariff must be above current 
YEKDEM tariffs in the absence of carbon revenue and assuming that initial investment figures 
are realized so that the investment will become  reasonable. The investme nt decision date is 
identified as 24-June-2020, corres ponding to the date of the si gned credit agreement, which 
marked the financial closure of the project. Prior to this, the municipal solid waste supply contract 
was signed on 06-March-2020, which initiated project development processes, including 
preparation of the Environmental Impact A ssessment (EIA) and preliminary feasibility 
evaluations. The initial version of the EIA wa s submitted on 19-August-2020 with approval 
received on 09 April 2021. Despite the formal fe asibility report being finalized after the credit 
agreement, preliminary internal assessments and technical evaluations were used to inform 
investment negotiations. In Türkiye, such pre-feas ibility analysis is sufficient to support project 
financing and investment decisions, particularly when backed by a municipal contract. No 
additional board or governmental approval was required prior to the signing of the credit 
agreement.

The project initially received an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) approval and generation 
license based on a theoretical installed capacity of 14.14 MWe, which represented the maximum 
envisaged capacity at the time of the preliminar y planning and permitting stages. This approach 
is common in large-scale energy projects, where permitting is sought for the upper ceiling of 
capacity to ensure future flexibility and avoid regulatory re-application in case of later scale-up. 
However, during the final investment decision-m aking process, detailed technical and financial 
feasibility studies led to a more conservative and realistic target of 9.898 MW e. This decision 
reflected the constraints related to feedstock availability, technology selection, and financial 
 
50 It must be noted that commissioning is a process that can take up to three months. During the commissioning and trial 
stages, the bacterial culture is gradually established to adapt to  the organic fraction of municipal solid waste (MSW). Due to 
waste disposal practices in Türkiye, MSW is often not source-separated, and the presence of toxic substances or inhibitors is 
not uncommon. The trial stage ensures that the bacterial culture can effectively process the incoming organic waste and 
produce biogas.

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
67 
viability. Subsequently, after further on-ground assessments and operational phase, the actual 
commissioned capacity was limited to 8.484 MW e. 
This staged reduction from 14.14 MW e to 9.898 MW e, and finally to 8.484 e MW, was driven by 
several key factors: 
 Limited feedstock supply: The biogas potential from the available organic waste streams did 
not have the capacity from sufficient biogas for full utilization of 14.14 MW e. 
 Investment constraints: Financing and capital availability supported an 8.484 MW e 
implementation, which offered a mo re sustainable financial model. 
 Grid interconnection and technical efficien cy: Optimal generator efficiency and O&M 
considerations favored a slightly smaller config uration. It must be noted that each new gas 
engines requires the installation of various su pport system and requires an investment of 
500,000 – 750,000 USD 51. 
The reduction from 9.898 MW e planned to 8.484 MW e commissioned does not result in a lower 
total electricity generation. The site has yielded an average of 5.5-7.5 MWe starting from the 2022 
where capacity is reached to 8.484 MWe52. This indicates there is an unutilized installed capacity. 
Since electricity generation is a primary income stream, the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is 
consequently affected. 
In the project's financial analysis, this reduction of electricity generation (in respect to ex-ante/ 
feasibility) is observed. While ex-ante IRR would not be effected from 8.484 MWe installed 
capacity (early version estimated high PLF and lo wer internal electricity consumption), the overall 
realized financial performance dropped significantly due to lower performance of all types of 
incomes (electricity sale, waste treatment, RDF sale). The integration of carbon revenue (VERs) 
remains critical to achieving the benchmark IRR. 
In conclusion, although the original EIA and license referenced 14.14 MW
e as a potential-looking 
capacity cap, the actual implementation was pragmatically scaled to 8.484 MW e based on real-
world constraints. This approach allows the projec t to operate within a financially and technically 
sustainable framework. 
Financial analysis is conducted with the values and assumptions from the feasibility report, dated 
14-June-2020.

in the IRR calculation the date specified in the tool was used.  
The World Bank has established a standard for renewable energy investments in Turkey. It has 
an IRR threshold benchmark, which is the minimum IRR necessary for project finance. As a result, 
it is a "commercial lending rate," as defined by Tool27. The benchmark IRR was derived from a 
World Bank loan to the Turkish Renewable Energy Sector under the Clean Technology Fund (CTF). 
The suggested CTF benchmark was found to be comp atible with the qualifying requirements for 
emission reduction projects (that is, significant potential in emission reductions, demonstration 
potential, development impact and implementati on potential). For each project type, threshold 
IRRs have been computed, which are the lowest IRRs required to attract investors. 
Based on an analysis of prototype sub-projects (see Table 3.3 page 40), it was determined that 
CTF resources would be made available for newer RE technologies and EE investments, and for 
smaller-scale hydro and wind projects, financial barriers existed which reduced the rates of return 
on equity (IRRs) below the levels required to attract investors. The desired thresholds were 
assessed as follows: 15% for small hydro, 20% biomass, and 25 % for solar.

Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of the İnegöl Integrated Solid Waste Storage and Disposal Facility 
has been calculated as 13.174% based on the parameters given above without considering the 
carbon revenue. The input values of all financial analysis are derived from the company feasibility 
report sheet which was completed by the project owner. Investment decision date was accepted 
as 24-June-2020, corresponding to the date of the signed credit agreement, which marked the 
financial closure of the project. As demonstrated feasibility sheet, operation starts in 2021 with 
2 units and another 4 un its commissioned up to 2023. Although licence capacity allows 
commissioning of 10 gas engines, current incoming waste amounts is not sufficient to support 
another gas engine. Waste management of Bursa province ( İnegöl is part of Bursa province) is 
decided by Bursa municipality and even with the increasing waste amount, plant load factor will 
increase. In the facility, several systems are involved where different revenues and expenses 
occur. In the IRR excel, under the “O&M” and “Revenue”, sheets, breakdown of costs and 
revenues can be found within operation years as RDF and Waste disposal facility revenues 
included in the equity IRR. The project does not use any ODA or government incentive. Electricity 
tariff has been used as $133 USD cent (¢)/kW h for 10 years according to YEKDEM mechanism 
starting from 2022 and after this period electricity price assumed to be spot price of market. 
Market spot price has been estimated as 134.026 (¢)/kWh (within feasibility report). This value 
is depending on PTF price in Turkey, and it is very volatile and unforeseeable which is determining 
related to supply – demand price on that specific  day. This price is almost impossible to make 
long term forecast because of unstable and unreliable economic environment in Turkey. This 
Tariff assumption is done technically. Additi onally, the project owner applied for the YEKDEM 
mechanism in October 2021 to benefit from the 2022 YEKDEM feed-in tariff. As per Regulation, 
request for registration to YEKDEM should be realized every October. Please see The IRR excel 
sheet for the demonstration of the calculation. 
The annual generation has been taken as 59.455 GWh as calculated in the feasibility sheet in 
line with generation license and commissioned units. This generation value by years are 
demonstrated in detail in IRR excel “Revenue (Feasibility)” sheet. In this excel sheet details of 
first year production are calculated. 
The project IRR of İnegöl Integrated Solid Waste Storage and Disposal Facility has been 
calculated on the basis of expected cash flows (investment, operating costs and revenues from 
electricity sale, waste disposal and RDF facility ), as determined in the financial feasibility 
assessment of the project. The parameters and values used for the IRR calculation will be 
provided to VCS DOE, if needed. The resulting IRR for 19 years is 13.174% 
Acceptable IRR values for energy investments in Turkey are, given the present global financial 
crisis and economic uncertainty, are expected at yields in excess of 20% per annum. Considering 
the market price risk, it can be concluded the extension will make the investment more feasible, 
however, it will still be below the benchmark IRR.

Sensitivity analysis was carried out for three main parameters identified for the first phase of the 
project. Since the investment cost of the project is not changed, only impact of change in tariff 
and operating cost has been included in sensitivity analysis. 
While the main parameter determining the income of  the project is the electricity sales revenue, 
investment cost and operation cost, a variation of the accordant values shall demonstrate the 
reliability of the IRR calculation. Applying variations on investment costs, the range was 
considered to be in a bandwidth of +10% and –10% as advised in “Tool27- Investment Analysis 
(ver 07.0).” 
The sensitivity analysis confirms th at the proposed project activity is unlikely to be economically 
attractive without the revenues from VER as even the maximum IRR result (12.432%) for the 
best-case scenario is below the benchmark, which is 10%. In this scenario, increase of revenue 
up to %10 is limited for electricity revenue by the local regulation discussed below in details. 
Electricity revenues have majority in the total revenues however other revenues are also effective 
where they depend on the waste amount which are fixed by agreements. Also, for electricity 
revenue, according to YEKDEM pricing, the fixed tariff is determined for a 10 years period. Since 
there is a fixed price and following to it ceiling price for renewable energy power plants, and waste 
amount which were already determined by agreements there is no chance that the revenue will 
be +10%. 
The project has already financed with project owne r equity and due to the fact that the project 
owner is the distributor of the electronic componen ts in Turkey so, own resources are used in this 
project that means there is no chance that the investment cost will be -%10. In addition to that, 
Turkey has feed in tariff which means fixed electricity price for renewable project, it is unlikely to 
have a higher electricity price, which is above the market price.

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2

The sensitivity analysis table outlines the impact of varying key parameters such as investment 
costs, operation and maintenance (O&M) costs, revenue, electricity generation, and tariff rates 
on the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of the project over a 19-year period. The variance applied in 
the sensitivity analysis ranges from -10% to +10% , allowing us to assess the project's financial 
robustness under fluctuating market and operational conditions. 
Investment Costs: The analysis shows that a 10% increase in investment costs leads to a 
decrease in the IRR to 10.858%, while a 10% re duction results in an increase to 15.945%. 
However, as the project has already completed a majority of investments, the likelihood of 
investment costs further decreasing is effectively zero. This further emphasizes that the project 
is already locked into a specific cost structure, making it challenging to improve the financial 
outcome through cost reduction. 
O&M Costs: Changes in O&M costs show a considerable impact on the IRR. A 10% increase in 
O&M costs results in a lower IRR of 8.293%, whil e a 10% decrease raises the IRR to 17.763%. 
The project’s current operational framework has built-in fixed operational costs that are unlikely 
to be reduced. This leaves limited room for improving profitability from O&M cost adjustments, 
further highlighting the project's sensit ivity to operational cost fluctuations. 
Revenue: As the primary determinant of the proj ect's income, fluctuations  in electricity sales 
revenue significantly influence the IRR. A 10% increase in revenue results in the IRR reaching 
17.880%, while a 10% decrease brings it down to 8.183. However, given the fixed feed-in tariff 
system and the latest ceiling price regulations imposed by the Turkish government, an increase 
in revenue beyond the current level is highly im probable. The sensitivity analysis confirms that 
the IRR improvement through revenue growth is constrained by regulatory caps, making the 
financial viability of the project heavily depend ent on the introduction of Verified Emission 
Reduction (VER) revenue. 
Breaching values derived from the sensitivity analysis have been crosschecked against the 
differences between ex-ante and realized expenses and revenue after 3 years of project 
operation. This process evaluates whether the proj ect still relies on carbon revenue for financial 
viability.

Another important parameter affecting IRR is in vestment cost. However, since the agreements

Likelihood of Reaching Breaching Values 
The "Breaching Value" column in the sensitivity analysis table provides a threshold where each 
parameter would need to fluctuate to meet the project's benchmark IRR of 20%. For instance, 
investment costs would need to decrease by 21.750%, O&M costs by 14.9900%, or revenue 
would need to increase by 14.630% to achieve a 20% IRR. Given the operational, regulatory, and 
market constraints discussed above, these breach ing values are extremely unlikely to occur in 
practice, reinforcing the assertion that the project is not economically viable without additional 
revenue from VERs. It has been demonstrated in the IRR analysis and summary of Table 12, 
realized values did not exceed the breaching values and remained within the limits.

In accordance with applied methodology and th e Tool-14, leakage emissions from digestate 
storage (LE storage) are excluded from the project’s leakage calculations. This is because neither 
the liquid nor the solid digestate is stored under anaerobic conditions. The liquid fraction of the 
digestate is either recycled back to the anaerobi c digestion reactors (approximately half of it) or 
sent directly to an aerated wastewater treatment plant (WWTP), ensuring aerobic treatment. The 
solid digestate is utilized as daily cover material at the landfill, applied in layers less than 1 meter 
thick, which does not meet the volumetric or an aerobic exposure conditio ns that define a solid 
waste disposal site (SWDS) per AM-Tool-14. Since digestate is handled in fully aerobic pathways 
and no anaerobic storage or unmanaged stockpiling occurs, LE
storage is conservatively set to zero.

and are determined as follows:

Determination of leakage emissions asso ciated with storage of digestate (LE storage,y) 
This step applies in the case that the digestate is stored under the following anaerobic conditions: 
(a) In an un-aerated lagoon that has a depth of more than one meter; or 
(b) In a SWDS, including stockpiles that ar e considered a SWDS as per the definitions 
section. 
Storage of digestate under anaerobic conditions can cause CH
4 emissions due to further 
anaerobic digestion of the residual biodegradabl e organic matter. The procedure for determining 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 is distinguished for liquid digestate and solid digestate. 
Determining LEstorage,y for liquid digestate 
Liquid digestate is mechanically separated and half  of it recycled back to anaerobic reactors for 
its nutrient content, other half is sent to aerobic wastewater treatment facility (WWTP). Due to 
aeration activities in the WWTP, this leakage source is accounted as zero. 
Determining LEstorage,y for solid digestate 
Where solid digestate is disposed in a SWDS or a stockpile that can be considered a SWDS, as 
per the definition section, then project participants may choose between Option 1 or Option 2 to 
determine 𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 as the TOOL14..  
Solid digestate will be sent to designated sludge area in the landfill first, then used as cover 
material (top layer due to material property) in the landfills thus covered under aerobic conditions.  
The solid digestate used as cover material in the landfill complies with ACM0022 applicability 
condition 1(j), which states that digestate is no t stored under anaerobic conditions on-site. The 
digestate is not stockpiled or stored in a way that allows anaerobic degradation.

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
87 
To address concerns about anaerobic decomposition and potential methane emissions, it is 
important to highlight that the digestate is fully stabilized during the anaerobic digestion process. 
This stabilization significantly reduces the volati le solids content, effectively removing readily 
biodegradable organic fractions and minimizing methane-generation potential. Furthermore, its 
application as a landfill cover ensures that it  remains in aerobic conditions. Because the 
digestate is applied as the top layer, it is co ntinuously exposed to atmospheric oxygen, which 
prevents the formation of anaerobic pockets. Given its low organic matter content and aerobic 
placement, the digestate does no t generate methane emissions. 
In conclusion, the application of solid digestate as landfill cover ensures full compliance with 
ACM0022 condition 1(j) and prevents anaerobi c decomposition. This leakage source is 
accounted as zero.

According to ACM0022 methodology, LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y is determined using “TOOL04: Emissions 
from solid waste disposal sites”. In the tool, x begins with the start of the CDM project activity and 
extends to the end of year y (e.g. emissions are calculated using Application B in the tool and waste 
disposed from the start of the first cred iting period shall be considered). And Wj,x in the tool is the 
amount of organic waste contained in the waste by-products from the production of RDF/SB in year 
y (e.g. it does not include waste by-products that  are composted instead of being disposed to a 
SWDS in the project activity or waste by-products from the combustion of RDF/SB). 
RDF production is solely based on using certai n fraction of municipal solid waste that is 
mechanically separated and mechanically processed on wet basis. Within mechanical separation 
processes, 2D wastes separated after ballistic separation recycl able materials are separated. 
Remaining material is converted into RDF and all of  it considered and expected to be sold to the

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
88 
end-user. Output material contains organic fracti on as well inorganic fraction (such as plastics, 
glass etc.). 
Based on this situation, no amount of by-product or trash is expected to be disposed of. Therefore  
(LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y) is considered zero. 
 
Leakage emissions associated with end use of RDF/SB exported outside the project boundary 
(LEENDUSE,RDF_SB,y ) 
According to ACM0022 methodology, the potential leakage emissions associated with the use of 
the RDF/SB that is exported outside the projec t boundary are that it may be combusted or 
decompose anaerobically has to be accounted for. Emissions are therefore calculated allowing for 
the situation that RDF/SB exported in year y may have three different end uses: 
 
 End use 1: documented evidence is provided that the RDF/SB exported off-site is used as 
raw material in fertilizer, ceramic manufactur e or as a fuel that is combusted in a CDM 
project activity. In this case, no leakage emissions are estimated; 
 End use 2: documented evidence is provided  that the RDF/SB exported off-site is 
combusted or used as a raw material in furnit ure: In this case, the RDF/SB is considered 
to be combusted and LEENDUSE,RDF_SB,y shall be calculated, according to procedure below; 
 End use 3: no documented evidence is provid ed that the off-site end use of RDF/SB is 
either combustion, furniture manufacture, fertilizer or ceramic production. In this case, the 
RDF/SB may decay anaerobically or be combusted. Therefore, it is conservatively assumed 
that the RDF/SB decays anaerobically according to the procedure below.  
𝐿𝐸ாே஽௎ௌா_ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬ ൌ𝑄ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,஼ைெ,௬  𝑥 𝑁𝐶𝑉ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬  𝑥 𝐸𝐹஼ைଶ_ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬  Equation (34) 
 
Where: 
 
LEENDUSE_RDF_SB,y   = Leakage emissions of CO 2 from off-site combustion of RDF/SB in year 
y (t CO2) 
QRDF_SB,COM,y   = Quantity of RDF/SB exported off-site with potential to be combusted 
in year y (t) 
EFCO2_RDF_SB,y   = CO 2 emissions factor for RDF/SB in year y (t CO 2/GJ) 
NCVRDF_SB,y  = Net calorific value of RDF/SB in year y (GJ/t) 
 Leakage emissions from anaerobic decay of RDF/SB are accounted by adjusting the 
quantity of organic waste used to produce RDF/SB (WRDF_SB,j,x) in the calculation of baseline 
emissions from solid waste disposal sites, as follows:

and procedures applied 
Two meters are placed (one main and one back-up) by Turkish 
Electricity Distribution Company (TEIAS) at the facility. These 
meters are TEIAS’s own property and can only be replaced or 
repaired by TEIAS. The design of the metering system is checked 
and approved by TEIAS before commissioning the plant. These 
meters are sealed by TEIAS and in tervention by project proponent 
is not possible. The fact that two meters are installed in a 
redundant manner keeps the un certainty level of the only 
parameter for baseline calculation low. High data quality of this 
parameter is not only in the inte rest of the emission reduction 
monitoring, but paramount for the business relation between the 
plant operator and the electricity buyers.  
Monthly settlement notifications of EPIAS consist hourly electricity 
production and withdrawn from the grid. 
The meters will be reading electricity supplied to the system and 
withdrawn from the system separately, the consumed electricity 
amount will be electricity withdrawn from the grid.
