Outcome of Step 1a: List of plausible alte rnative scenarios to the project activity 
There is no national regulation which would require the recovery or flaring of the LFG emitted 
from landfill sites. The relevant environmental legislation does not force municipalities to 
effectively control LFG emissions from landfills; henc e, it is rather a tender for the rights to use 
the site for power generation purposes. The LFG would be partially captured and flared. Thus, 
baseline scenario M1, M2  and M3 are applicable.

text_blocks.heading: P6 is a plausible scenario for the project activity. There was no renewable based electricity plant
text_blocks.text:
on-site before the project activity. 
Step 1b: Consistency with mandator y applicable laws and regulations 
All the credible alternatives identified above co mply with Turkey's current laws and regulatory 
requirements. The project activity is not the only  alternative scenario that follows all mandatory 
regulations with which there is general compliance. 
Step 2: Barrier Analysis 
There are no technology barriers, acceptability barriers nor financial barriers that may prevent 
these two alternative scenarios from occurring. As per Tool 02 Combined tool to identify the 
baseline scenario and demonstrate additionality (Version 07.0), go to Step 3 investment analysis. 
STEP 3 – Investment analysis;  
The investment analysis has been done in order to make an economic and financial evaluation 
of the project. No public funding or ODA are available in Turkey for finance of this type of projects. 
For investment analysis, loan conditions have been determined considering the average market 
rates/term sheets signed with the banks. Investment analysis is carried as per the 
methodological Tool 2: “Combined tool to identify the baseline scenario and demonstrate 
additionality”, Version 07.0.  
Sub-step 2a - Determine appropriate analysis method 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
65 
There are three options for the determin ation of analysis method which are: 
 Simple Cost Analysis 
 Investment Comparison Analysis and 
 Benchmark Analysis 
Since Project generates economic benefits from sales of electricity, the simple cost analysis is 
not applicable. Also, since the baseline of the project is generation of electricity by the grid, no 
alternative investment is considered at issue. So , it has been decided to use benchmark analysis 
for evaluation of the project investment. 
Sub-step 2b: Option III. Apply benchmark analysis 
For benchmark analysis, figure defined by World Bank for similar project types have been used 
which has been given as 20% (pre-tax) 49 for IRR by a report generated in June 2017. For the 
proposed project, in order to reach this IRR values, average electricity tariff must be above current 
YEKDEM tariffs in the absence of carbon revenue and assuming that initial investment figures 
are realized so that the investment will become  reasonable. The investme nt decision date is 
identified as 24-June-2020, corres ponding to the date of the si gned credit agreement, which 
marked the financial closure of the project. Prior to this, the municipal solid waste supply contract 
was signed on 06-March-2020, which initiated project development processes, including 
preparation of the Environmental Impact A ssessment (EIA) and preliminary feasibility 
evaluations. The initial version of the EIA wa s submitted on 19-August-2020 with approval 
received on 09 April 2021. Despite the formal fe asibility report being finalized after the credit 
agreement, preliminary internal assessments and technical evaluations were used to inform 
investment negotiations. In Türkiye, such pre-feas ibility analysis is sufficient to support project 
financing and investment decisions, particularly when backed by a municipal contract. No 
additional board or governmental approval was required prior to the signing of the credit 
agreement.

The project initially received an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) approval and generation 
license based on a theoretical installed capacity of 14.14 MWe, which represented the maximum 
envisaged capacity at the time of the preliminar y planning and permitting stages. This approach 
is common in large-scale energy projects, where permitting is sought for the upper ceiling of 
capacity to ensure future flexibility and avoid regulatory re-application in case of later scale-up. 
However, during the final investment decision-m aking process, detailed technical and financial 
feasibility studies led to a more conservative and realistic target of 9.898 MW e. This decision 
reflected the constraints related to feedstock availability, technology selection, and financial

text_blocks.heading: 50 It must be noted that commissioning is a process that can take up to three months. During the commissioning and trial
text_blocks.text:
stages, the bacterial culture is gradually established to adapt to  the organic fraction of municipal solid waste (MSW). Due to 
waste disposal practices in Türkiye, MSW is often not source-separated, and the presence of toxic substances or inhibitors is 
not uncommon. The trial stage ensures that the bacterial culture can effectively process the incoming organic waste and 
produce biogas. 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
67 
viability. Subsequently, after further on-ground assessments and operational phase, the actual 
commissioned capacity was limited to 8.484 MW e. 
This staged reduction from 14.14 MW e to 9.898 MW e, and finally to 8.484 e MW, was driven by 
several key factors: 
 Limited feedstock supply: The biogas potential from the available organic waste streams did 
not have the capacity from sufficient biogas for full utilization of 14.14 MW e. 
 Investment constraints: Financing and capital availability supported an 8.484 MW e 
implementation, which offered a mo re sustainable financial model. 
 Grid interconnection and technical efficien cy: Optimal generator efficiency and O&M 
considerations favored a slightly smaller config uration. It must be noted that each new gas 
engines requires the installation of various su pport system and requires an investment of 
500,000 – 750,000 USD 51. 
The reduction from 9.898 MW e planned to 8.484 MW e commissioned does not result in a lower 
total electricity generation. The site has yielded an average of 5.5-7.5 MWe starting from the 2022 
where capacity is reached to 8.484 MWe52. This indicates there is an unutilized installed capacity. 
Since electricity generation is a primary income stream, the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is 
consequently affected. 
In the project's financial analysis, this reduction of electricity generation (in respect to ex-ante/ 
feasibility) is observed. While ex-ante IRR would not be effected from 8.484 MWe installed 
capacity (early version estimated high PLF and lo wer internal electricity consumption), the overall 
realized financial performance dropped significantly due to lower performance of all types of 
incomes (electricity sale, waste treatment, RDF sale). The integration of carbon revenue (VERs) 
remains critical to achieving the benchmark IRR. 
In conclusion, although the original EIA and license referenced 14.14 MW
e as a potential-looking 
capacity cap, the actual implementation was pragmatically scaled to 8.484 MW e based on real-
world constraints. This approach allows the projec t to operate within a financially and technically 
sustainable framework. 
Financial analysis is conducted with the values and assumptions from the feasibility report, dated 
14-June-2020.

text_blocks.text:
in the IRR calculation the date specified in the tool was used.  
The World Bank has established a standard for renewable energy investments in Turkey. It has 
an IRR threshold benchmark, which is the minimum IRR necessary for project finance. As a result, 
it is a "commercial lending rate," as defined by Tool27. The benchmark IRR was derived from a 
World Bank loan to the Turkish Renewable Energy Sector under the Clean Technology Fund (CTF). 
The suggested CTF benchmark was found to be comp atible with the qualifying requirements for 
emission reduction projects (that is, significant potential in emission reductions, demonstration 
potential, development impact and implementati on potential). For each project type, threshold 
IRRs have been computed, which are the lowest IRRs required to attract investors. 
Based on an analysis of prototype sub-projects (see Table 3.3 page 40), it was determined that 
CTF resources would be made available for newer RE technologies and EE investments, and for 
smaller-scale hydro and wind projects, financial barriers existed which reduced the rates of return 
on equity (IRRs) below the levels required to attract investors. The desired thresholds were 
assessed as follows: 15% for small hydro, 20% biomass, and 25 % for solar.

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
69 
Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of the İnegöl Integrated Solid Waste Storage and Disposal Facility 
has been calculated as 13.174% based on the parameters given above without considering the 
carbon revenue. The input values of all financial analysis are derived from the company feasibility 
report sheet which was completed by the project owner. Investment decision date was accepted 
as 24-June-2020, corresponding to the date of the signed credit agreement, which marked the 
financial closure of the project. As demonstrated feasibility sheet, operation starts in 2021 with 
2 units and another 4 un its commissioned up to 2023. Although licence capacity allows 
commissioning of 10 gas engines, current incoming waste amounts is not sufficient to support 
another gas engine. Waste management of Bursa province ( İnegöl is part of Bursa province) is 
decided by Bursa municipality and even with the increasing waste amount, plant load factor will 
increase. In the facility, several systems are involved where different revenues and expenses 
occur. In the IRR excel, under the “O&M” and “Revenue”, sheets, breakdown of costs and 
revenues can be found within operation years as RDF and Waste disposal facility revenues 
included in the equity IRR. The project does not use any ODA or government incentive. Electricity 
tariff has been used as $133 USD cent (¢)/kW h for 10 years according to YEKDEM mechanism 
starting from 2022 and after this period electricity price assumed to be spot price of market. 
Market spot price has been estimated as 134.026 (¢)/kWh (within feasibility report). This value 
is depending on PTF price in Turkey, and it is very volatile and unforeseeable which is determining 
related to supply – demand price on that specific  day. This price is almost impossible to make 
long term forecast because of unstable and unreliable economic environment in Turkey. This 
Tariff assumption is done technically. Additi onally, the project owner applied for the YEKDEM 
mechanism in October 2021 to benefit from the 2022 YEKDEM feed-in tariff. As per Regulation, 
request for registration to YEKDEM should be realized every October. Please see The IRR excel 
sheet for the demonstration of the calculation. 
The annual generation has been taken as 59.455 GWh as calculated in the feasibility sheet in 
line with generation license and commissioned units. This generation value by years are 
demonstrated in detail in IRR excel “Revenue (Feasibility)” sheet. In this excel sheet details of 
first year production are calculated. 
The project IRR of İnegöl Integrated Solid Waste Storage and Disposal Facility has been 
calculated on the basis of expected cash flows (investment, operating costs and revenues from 
electricity sale, waste disposal and RDF facility ), as determined in the financial feasibility 
assessment of the project. The parameters and values used for the IRR calculation will be 
provided to VCS DOE, if needed. The resulting IRR for 19 years is 13.174% 
Acceptable IRR values for energy investments in Turkey are, given the present global financial 
crisis and economic uncertainty, are expected at yields in excess of 20% per annum. Considering 
the market price risk, it can be concluded the extension will make the investment more feasible, 
however, it will still be below the benchmark IRR. 
Sub-step 2d – Sensitivity Analysis 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
70 
Sensitivity analysis was carried out for three main parameters identified for the first phase of the 
project. Since the investment cost of the project is not changed, only impact of change in tariff 
and operating cost has been included in sensitivity analysis. 
While the main parameter determining the income of  the project is the electricity sales revenue, 
investment cost and operation cost, a variation of the accordant values shall demonstrate the 
reliability of the IRR calculation. Applying variations on investment costs, the range was 
considered to be in a bandwidth of +10% and –10% as advised in “Tool27- Investment Analysis 
(ver 07.0).” 
The sensitivity analysis confirms th at the proposed project activity is unlikely to be economically 
attractive without the revenues from VER as even the maximum IRR result (12.432%) for the 
best-case scenario is below the benchmark, which is 10%. In this scenario, increase of revenue 
up to %10 is limited for electricity revenue by the local regulation discussed below in details. 
Electricity revenues have majority in the total revenues however other revenues are also effective 
where they depend on the waste amount which are fixed by agreements. Also, for electricity 
revenue, according to YEKDEM pricing, the fixed tariff is determined for a 10 years period. Since 
there is a fixed price and following to it ceiling price for renewable energy power plants, and waste 
amount which were already determined by agreements there is no chance that the revenue will 
be +10%. 
The project has already financed with project owne r equity and due to the fact that the project 
owner is the distributor of the electronic componen ts in Turkey so, own resources are used in this 
project that means there is no chance that the investment cost will be -%10. In addition to that, 
Turkey has feed in tariff which means fixed electricity price for renewable project, it is unlikely to 
have a higher electricity price, which is above the market price.

 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
71 
The sensitivity analysis table outlines the impact of varying key parameters such as investment 
costs, operation and maintenance (O&M) costs, revenue, electricity generation, and tariff rates 
on the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of the project over a 19-year period. The variance applied in 
the sensitivity analysis ranges from -10% to +10% , allowing us to assess the project's financial 
robustness under fluctuating market and operational conditions. 
Investment Costs: The analysis shows that a 10% increase in investment costs leads to a 
decrease in the IRR to 10.858%, while a 10% re duction results in an increase to 15.945%. 
However, as the project has already completed a majority of investments, the likelihood of 
investment costs further decreasing is effectively zero. This further emphasizes that the project 
is already locked into a specific cost structure, making it challenging to improve the financial 
outcome through cost reduction. 
O&M Costs: Changes in O&M costs show a considerable impact on the IRR. A 10% increase in 
O&M costs results in a lower IRR of 8.293%, whil e a 10% decrease raises the IRR to 17.763%. 
The project’s current operational framework has built-in fixed operational costs that are unlikely 
to be reduced. This leaves limited room for improving profitability from O&M cost adjustments, 
further highlighting the project's sensit ivity to operational cost fluctuations. 
Revenue: As the primary determinant of the proj ect's income, fluctuations  in electricity sales 
revenue significantly influence the IRR. A 10% increase in revenue results in the IRR reaching 
17.880%, while a 10% decrease brings it down to 8.183. However, given the fixed feed-in tariff 
system and the latest ceiling price regulations imposed by the Turkish government, an increase 
in revenue beyond the current level is highly im probable. The sensitivity analysis confirms that 
the IRR improvement through revenue growth is constrained by regulatory caps, making the 
financial viability of the project heavily depend ent on the introduction of Verified Emission 
Reduction (VER) revenue. 
Breaching values derived from the sensitivity analysis have been crosschecked against the 
differences between ex-ante and realized expenses and revenue after 3 years of project 
operation. This process evaluates whether the proj ect still relies on carbon revenue for financial 
viability.

The investment and sensitivity analysis shows th at the VER revenues will improve the financial

According to local regulations, electricity price is  determined daily accord ing to Market Financial 
Settlement Centre (MFSC) as defined in the regulations and there exists three tariffs during day, 
peak and night hours. The value does not provide any guarantee about the actual selling price 
as the control on generation period and tariff is limited and it may not be possible to generate 
and sell electricity during peak tariff periods. Also, considering that fluctuation and dependence 
in sunshine is high and fact that a part of the electricity can be sold through bilateral agreements 
to free consumers with a discount rate over ma rket price, guarantee price has been taken as 
reference in investment analysis which also provides input for evaluation of financing institutions. 
However, the latest regulation will have inhibitor effect on renewable energy-based project as

Another important parameter affecting IRR is in vestment cost. However, since the agreements 
have been made and costs are realized as given in the financial model, there is no chance of 
expecting a decrease in the invest ment cost due to the inflation rate which also considered in 
the feasibility report. Operating costs can also affect the IRR. However, its impact is not significant 
and does not cause any significant change in IRR, and the fluctuation percentage to reach the 
benchmark is very high and not likely. Based on the above information, it is seen that project is 
not the most attractive option. Therefore, the project is considered an addition to the baseline 
scenario. 
Likelihood of Reaching Breaching Values 
The "Breaching Value" column in the sensitivity analysis table provides a threshold where each 
parameter would need to fluctuate to meet the project's benchmark IRR of 20%. For instance, 
investment costs would need to decrease by 21.750%, O&M costs by 14.9900%, or revenue 
would need to increase by 14.630% to achieve a 20% IRR. Given the operational, regulatory, and 
market constraints discussed above, these breach ing values are extremely unlikely to occur in 
practice, reinforcing the assertion that the project is not economically viable without additional 
revenue from VERs. It has been demonstrated in the IRR analysis and summary of Table 12, 
realized values did not exceed the breaching values and remained within the limits. 
Step 4: Common Practice Analysis  
The section below provides the Common Practice Analysis as step 4 of the “Combined tool to 
identify the baseline scenario and demonstrate additionality, version 7.0” and “Common Practice, 
version 03.1”.  
Step 1: Calculate applicable capacity or output range as +/- 50% of the total design capacity or 
output of the proposed project activity  
The project’s installed capacity as per feasibility report is 9.898 MW
e. Therefore, the applicable 
capacity range for the analysis will be 4.949 MW e – 14.847 MWe. 
Step 2: Identify similar projects (both CDM and non-CDM) which fulfill all of the following 
conditions:  
(a) The projects are located in the applicable geographical area;  
(b) The projects apply the same measur e as the proposed project activity; 
(c) The projects use the same energy source/fuel and feedstock as the proposed project activity, 
if a technology switch measure is implemented by the proposed project activity;  
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
74 
(d) The plants in which the projects are implemented produce goods or services with comparable 
quality, properties, and applications areas (e.g. clinker) as the proposed project plant;  
(e) The capacity or output of the projects is within the applicable capacity or output range 
calculated in Step 1;  
(f) The projects started commercial operation before the project design document (CDM-PDD) is 
published for global stakeholder consultation or before the start date of proposed project activity, 
whichever is earlier for the proposed project activity.  
Step 3: Within the projects identified in Step 2, identify those that are neither registered CDM 
project activities, project activities submitted for registration, nor project activities undergoing 
validation. Note their number N
all. 
 
Step-2 (a to f) and Step 3: 
 
The start date of the Project Activity is 31-December-2020. Therefore, projects which have started 
commercial operation before 31-December-2020, have been considered for analysis. The most 
up-to-date list of the plants operating in Turkey have been developed by adding new projects until

published by The Ministry of En ergy and Natural Resources in Turkey from year 2012. Since the 
project activity produces electricity, electric ity generation companies are considered for the 
analysis. The plants whose capacities are between 4.949 MW
e – 14.847 MWe were included. The 
projects which have the same measure and company type are included in the list (biomass based

projects and projects under validation are excluded. The number of remaining power plants, Nall, 
is 2 9.  As  st a t ed  in  St e p 2-  c)  chose n  lis t  o nly consists of energy projects since in the step 2 
calculation only renewable energy sources in Turkey taken since there is no technology switch 
measure implementation, this step is not applic able. Both Nall and Similar projects list are 
displayed in Appendix 3 and 4. 
Step 4: within similar projects identified in Step 3, identify those that apply technologies that are 
different to the technology applied in the proposed project activity. Note their number N diff.  
All projects that use MSW are similar projects. There are five (5) similar projects.  
Thus, Ndiff = 29-5=24. 
Step 5: calculate factor F = 1 - N diff / Nall representing the share of similar projects (penetration 
rate of the measure/technology) using a measure/technology similar to the measure/technology 
used in the proposed project activity that deliver the same output or capacity as the proposed 
project activity. 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
75 
F = 1 - (Ndiff / Nall)  
F = 1 – (24/29) = 0.172 
Nall - Ndiff = 29 - 24 = 5 
The proposed project activity is a “common pr actice” within a sector in the applicable 
geographical area if the factor F is greater than 0.2 and N all-Ndiff is greater than 3. This indicates 
that both conditions have to be met for project being common practice. 
Since Nall - Ndiff = 5 and F=0.172 , the project activity is not a common practice and so, the project 
is additional.

Project Emissions from fossil fuel consumption associated with the anaerobic digester in  (PEFC,y) 
Heat requirement of anaerobic digesters is supplied with waste heat from gas engines on site, 
Therefore, there is no fossil fuel consumption a ssociated with the anaerobic digesters. However, 
according to regulation there is to have a capt ive power plant or diesel generator in case of 
shortage on methane for electricity production in  case the electricity needs to be maintained, 
and the engines don’t provide it temporarily. It also partially powers the reactors (only necessary 
systems) to keep biological system ongoing. 
CO

text_blocks.heading: 4.3 Leakage
text_blocks.text:
Leakage emissions are associated  with anaerobic digestion and the use of RDF/SB that is 
exported outside the project boundary. Leakage emissions are determined as follows 
(ACM0022): 
𝐿𝐸
௬ ൌ𝐿 𝐸஼ைெ௉,௬൅𝐿 𝐸஺஽,௬൅𝐿 𝐸ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬ Equation (1) 
 
Where: 
𝐿𝐸௬ = Leakage emissions in the year y (t CO2e) 
𝐿𝐸஼ைெ௉,௬ = Leakage emissions from composting or co-composting in year y 
(t CO2e) 
𝐿𝐸஺஽,௬ = Leakage emissions from an aerobic digester in year y (t CO2e) 
𝐿𝐸ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬ = Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB in year y (t CO2e) 
 
Since there is no composting activity under project activity, Leakage emissions associated with 
composting ( LECOMP,y) is considered zero”. Leakage em issions associated with anaerobic 
digestion of waste (LE AD,y) is calculated in accordance with according to “TOOL14: Project and 
leakage emissions from anaerobic digesters” (Version 02.0). and LE RDF_SB,y are considered for 
the project activity.  
In accordance with applied methodology and th e Tool-14, leakage emissions from digestate 
storage (LE storage) are excluded from the project’s leakage calculations. This is because neither 
the liquid nor the solid digestate is stored under anaerobic conditions. The liquid fraction of the 
digestate is either recycled back to the anaerobi c digestion reactors (approximately half of it) or 
sent directly to an aerated wastewater treatment plant (WWTP), ensuring aerobic treatment. The 
solid digestate is utilized as daily cover material at the landfill, applied in layers less than 1 meter 
thick, which does not meet the volumetric or an aerobic exposure conditio ns that define a solid 
waste disposal site (SWDS) per AM-Tool-14. Since digestate is handled in fully aerobic pathways 
and no anaerobic storage or unmanaged stockpiling occurs, LE
storage is conservatively set to zero. 
Leakage emissions from anaerobic digestion (LE AD,y) 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
86 
Leakage emissions associated wi th the anaerobic digester ( 𝐿𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦) depend on how the digestate 
is managed. They include emissions associated with storage and compos ting of the digestate 
and are determined as follows: 
𝐿𝐸஺஽,௬ ൌ 𝐿𝐸௦௧௢௥௔௚௘,௬ ൅ 𝐿𝐸௖௢௠௣,௬ Equation (5) 
 
Where: 
𝐿𝐸஺஽,௬ = Leakage emissions from an aerobic digester in year y (tCO2e) 
𝐿𝐸௦௧௢௥௔௚௘,௬ = 
Leakage emissions associated with storage of digestate in year y 
(tCO2e) 
𝐿𝐸௖௢௠௣,௬ = 
Leakage emissions associated with composting digestate in year y 
(tCO2e) 
Determination of leakage emissions asso ciated with storage of digestate (LE storage,y) 
This step applies in the case that the digestate is stored under the following anaerobic conditions: 
(a) In an un-aerated lagoon that has a depth of more than one meter; or 
(b) In a SWDS, including stockpiles that ar e considered a SWDS as per the definitions 
section. 
Storage of digestate under anaerobic conditions can cause CH
4 emissions due to further 
anaerobic digestion of the residual biodegradabl e organic matter. The procedure for determining 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 is distinguished for liquid digestate and solid digestate. 
Determining LEstorage,y for liquid digestate 
Liquid digestate is mechanically separated and half  of it recycled back to anaerobic reactors for 
its nutrient content, other half is sent to aerobic wastewater treatment facility (WWTP). Due to 
aeration activities in the WWTP, this leakage source is accounted as zero. 
Determining LEstorage,y for solid digestate 
Where solid digestate is disposed in a SWDS or a stockpile that can be considered a SWDS, as 
per the definition section, then project participants may choose between Option 1 or Option 2 to 
determine 𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 as the TOOL14..  
Solid digestate will be sent to designated sludge area in the landfill first, then used as cover 
material (top layer due to material property) in the landfills thus covered under aerobic conditions.  
The solid digestate used as cover material in the landfill complies with ACM0022 applicability 
condition 1(j), which states that digestate is no t stored under anaerobic conditions on-site. The 
digestate is not stockpiled or stored in a way that allows anaerobic degradation. 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
87 
To address concerns about anaerobic decomposition and potential methane emissions, it is 
important to highlight that the digestate is fully stabilized during the anaerobic digestion process. 
This stabilization significantly reduces the volati le solids content, effectively removing readily 
biodegradable organic fractions and minimizing methane-generation potential. Furthermore, its 
application as a landfill cover ensures that it  remains in aerobic conditions. Because the 
digestate is applied as the top layer, it is co ntinuously exposed to atmospheric oxygen, which 
prevents the formation of anaerobic pockets. Given its low organic matter content and aerobic 
placement, the digestate does no t generate methane emissions. 
In conclusion, the application of solid digestate as landfill cover ensures full compliance with 
ACM0022 condition 1(j) and prevents anaerobi c decomposition. This leakage source is 
accounted as zero. 
Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB (LE
RDF_SB,y) 
Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB are accounted for the organic waste by-products of 
the treatment process (not by-products from the RDF/SB combustor), which may be composted 
or disposed of in a SWDS, and the end-use of RD F/SB that is exported off-site, as follows: 
𝐿𝐸
ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬ൌ𝐿 𝐸ாே஽௎ௌா_ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬൅𝐿 𝐸ௌௐ஽ௌ,ௐ஻௉_ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬ Equation (33) 
 
Where: 
LERDF_SB,y  = Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB in year y (t CO 2e) 
LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y  = Leakage emissions associated with disposing of waste by products 
associated with RDF/SB producti on in a SWDS in year y (t CO 2e) 
LEENDUSE_RDF_SB,y   = Leakage emissions associated with the end-use of RDF/SB exported 
outside the project boundary in year y (t CO 2e) 
Leakage emissions from disposal of waste by-products from RDF/SB production in a SWDS 
(LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y ) 
According to ACM0022 methodology, LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y is determined using “TOOL04: Emissions 
from solid waste disposal sites”. In the tool, x begins with the start of the CDM project activity and 
extends to the end of year y (e.g. emissions are calculated using Application B in the tool and waste 
disposed from the start of the first cred iting period shall be considered). And Wj,x in the tool is the 
amount of organic waste contained in the waste by-products from the production of RDF/SB in year 
y (e.g. it does not include waste by-products that  are composted instead of being disposed to a 
SWDS in the project activity or waste by-products from the combustion of RDF/SB). 
RDF production is solely based on using certai n fraction of municipal solid waste that is 
mechanically separated and mechanically processed on wet basis. Within mechanical separation 
processes, 2D wastes separated after ballistic separation recycl able materials are separated. 
Remaining material is converted into RDF and all of  it considered and expected to be sold to the 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
88 
end-user. Output material contains organic fracti on as well inorganic fraction (such as plastics, 
glass etc.). 
Based on this situation, no amount of by-product or trash is expected to be disposed of. Therefore  
(LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y) is considered zero. 
 
Leakage emissions associated with end use of RDF/SB exported outside the project boundary 
(LEENDUSE,RDF_SB,y ) 
According to ACM0022 methodology, the potential leakage emissions associated with the use of 
the RDF/SB that is exported outside the projec t boundary are that it may be combusted or 
decompose anaerobically has to be accounted for. Emissions are therefore calculated allowing for 
the situation that RDF/SB exported in year y may have three different end uses: 
 
 End use 1: documented evidence is provided that the RDF/SB exported off-site is used as 
raw material in fertilizer, ceramic manufactur e or as a fuel that is combusted in a CDM 
project activity. In this case, no leakage emissions are estimated; 
 End use 2: documented evidence is provided  that the RDF/SB exported off-site is 
combusted or used as a raw material in furnit ure: In this case, the RDF/SB is considered 
to be combusted and LEENDUSE,RDF_SB,y shall be calculated, according to procedure below; 
 End use 3: no documented evidence is provid ed that the off-site end use of RDF/SB is 
either combustion, furniture manufacture, fertilizer or ceramic production. In this case, the 
RDF/SB may decay anaerobically or be combusted. Therefore, it is conservatively assumed 
that the RDF/SB decays anaerobically according to the procedure below.  
𝐿𝐸ாே஽௎ௌா_ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬ ൌ𝑄ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,஼ைெ,௬  𝑥 𝑁𝐶𝑉ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬  𝑥 𝐸𝐹஼ைଶ_ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬  Equation (34) 
 
Where: 
 
LEENDUSE_RDF_SB,y   = Leakage emissions of CO 2 from off-site combustion of RDF/SB in year 
y (t CO2) 
QRDF_SB,COM,y   = Quantity of RDF/SB exported off-site with potential to be combusted 
in year y (t) 
EFCO2_RDF_SB,y   = CO 2 emissions factor for RDF/SB in year y (t CO 2/GJ) 
NCVRDF_SB,y  = Net calorific value of RDF/SB in year y (GJ/t)

quantity of organic waste used to produce RDF/SB (WRDF_SB,j,x) in the calculation of baseline 
emissions from solid waste disposal sites, as follows:

89

𝑄ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௬
ൈ𝑊ோ஽ி_ௌ஻,௝,௫ Equation (35) 
 
Where:

using the waste to produce RDF/SB in the year x, adjusted by the proportion of RDF/SB that is 
disposed of in a SWDS (t)

anaerobically in year y (t)

using the waste to produce RDF/SB in the year x (t) 
Current activity is the utilization of RDF under gl obal carbon council projects Cine Biomass Power

Project Description: VCS Version 4.2

Description Electricity generated by the alternative waste treatment process t 
and exported to the grid or displacing fossil fuel fired power-only 
and/or cogeneration captive energy generation in year y 
Source of data On-site measurements, EPIAS records 
Description of 
measurement methods 
and procedures applied 
Two meters are placed (one main and one back-up) by Turkish 
Electricity Distribution Company (TEIAS) at the facility. These 
meters are TEIAS’s own property and can only be replaced or 
repaired by TEIAS. The design of the metering system is checked 
and approved by TEIAS before commissioning the plant. These 
meters are sealed by TEIAS and in tervention by project proponent 
is not possible. The fact that two meters are installed in a 
redundant manner keeps the un certainty level of the only 
parameter for baseline calculation low. High data quality of this 
parameter is not only in the inte rest of the emission reduction 
monitoring, but paramount for the business relation between the 
plant operator and the electricity buyers.  
Monthly settlement notifications of EPIAS consist hourly electricity 
production and withdrawn from the grid. 
The meters will be reading electricity supplied to the system and 
withdrawn from the system separately, the consumed electricity 
amount will be electricity withdrawn from the grid. 
Frequency of 
monitoring/recording 
Continuous measurement, monthly readings

According to the Article 2 of the Communiqué of Meters in 
Electricity Sector: ‘The meters to be used in the electricity market 
shall be compliant with the standards of Turkish Standards 
Institute or IEC and have obtained “Type and System Approval” 
certificate from the Ministry of Trade and Industry.’ Therefore, the 
Ministry of Trade and Industry (Ministry) is responsible for control 
and calibration of the meters.  
Paragraph b) of the Article 9 of the Regulation of Metering and 
Testing of Metering Systems “Periodi c tests of meters of electricity, 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
107 
water, coal gas, natural gas and current and voltage transformers 
are done every 10 years.” Therefore, periodic calibration of the 
meters will be done every 10 years. 
According to Article 3 of System  Usage Agreement done by the 
Project Proponent and TEIAS; other than periodic tests, if a party 
alleges the meters are not working appropriately tests of the 
meters will be done by presence of both parties. If, after controls, 
it is seen that the meter is not working appropriately, the 
measurements of reserve meters are taken into account beginning 
from date both meters are reading the same (page 3, 2-c)

Data / Parameter Opj,h

Description Operation of the equipment that consumes the LFG  
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
109 
Source of data Project participants  
Description of 
measurement methods 
and procedures applied 
For each equipment unit j using the LFG monitor that the plant is 
operating in hour  h by the monitoring any one or more of the 
following three parameters: 
Temperature. Determine the location for temperature 
measurements and minimum oper ational temperature based on 
manufacturer’s specifications of the burning equipment. 
Document and justify the location  and minimum threshold in the 
PDD; 
Flame. Flame detection system is used to ensure that the 
equipment is in operation; 
Products generated. Monitor the generation 
Opj,h = 0 when: 
One of more temperature measurements are missing below the 
minimum threshold in hour h (i nstantaneous measurements are 
made at least every minute); 
Flame is not detected continuously in hour h (instantaneous 
measurements are made at least every minute) 
Otherwise, O pj,h = 1

text_blocks.heading: 5.3 Monitoring Plan
text_blocks.text:
The responsible entity for the monitoring system is Biotrend personnel. The monitoring activities will 
primarily involve four types of personnel: the Operation Site Manager, Field Operator, Electrical & 
Mechanical Operator, and the Laboratory Operator.  
The Field Operators will perform activities such as checking operations of the blower and flare, recording 
data at the blower/flare station, routine maintenance of collection system components, preparing daily 
logs and completing checklists, and send data to the Operation Site Manager.  
The Operation Manager’s responsibilities include reviewing the data collected both manually by the Field 
Technicians and the one recorded automatically by analytical equipment, making recommendations 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
126 
and/or implementing system adjustments to maxim ize methane capture and destruction, scheduling 
monitoring and O&M activities, performing quality assurance checks on operations, coordinating with 
system component manufacturers as needed, to ma intain proper operations and calibration, and 
compiling data as required by the Methodology.  
Only for manual data collection, the Operation Manager will be responsible for reviewing the data 
collected.  
Project Management Responsibility  
The operating and maintenance personnel will be sk illed technicians, with extensive experience in 
equipment operation, maintenance and calibration, and emergency procedures. Overall responsibility for 
the monitoring and maintenance of all required tasks and their adequate management lie with the project 
manager. Detailed roles and responsibilities of the re levant staff involved in VCS monitoring are placed.  
Training of Monitoring Personnel 
The monitoring personnel are trained in the beginning of the project; the purpose of this training is to 
operate the project in a well manner. All personnel are subjected to training shortly after joining the 
company. Periodical training usually conducted once a year to refresh information however interval is 
defined by Biotrend. 
 
Figure 6. Organizational Structure of the Carbon Project 
Data Analysis 
The collected data will be reviewed and analyzed on a daily basis by the Operation Manager. In case of a 
drift of one parameter the Manager or Technician ca n react quickly to fix potential problems. All data 
required for the emission reduction calculations wi ll be kept in the onsite- monitoring database. The 
Monitoring Manager will be responsible for reporting the necessary information to Biotrend. 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
127 
The quality actions that will guarantee the success of the monitoring plan are the following: 
Maintenance Plan 
The plant's computerized management and control system includes: the management of level 
management in gas meters through the level indicato rs; monitoring and control of the heating system; 
alarm management of minimum and maximum levels and security. With regards to the electricity 
generation component of the project, given that it dispatches electricity to the National Grid, measuring, 
recording, storing, aggregating, collating, and reporting data and parameters will follow the procedure 
described by the authorities. 
The following aspects are core to the maintenance of the monitoring system in order to assure proper 
data monitoring during the project: 
 Equipment preventive maintenance  
 Equipment calibration 
All data collected as part of monitoring will be arch ived electronically and be kept at least for 2 years 
after the end of the last crediting period. Regarding the monitoring equipment of the methane recovery 
component of the project, maintenance and calibration will be performed in line with manufacturers’ 
recommendations. With regards to the electricity meter,  it is a high accuracy measurement device/s and 
meets all relevant metrological requirements pres cribed by the state authority. Procedures for 
maintenance of the meter will be conducted in accordance with national procedures and standards.  
Quality assurance and Control (QA/QC)  
Quality control and quality assurance procedures will guarantee the quality of monitored data.  
All data is archived electronically, backed up regularly and kept at least for 2 years after the end of the 
last crediting period. There are corrective actions wh en the parameters are out of the permitted range.  
Regarding the monitoring equipment of the methane recovery component of the project, maintenance 
and calibration will be performed in line with ma nufacturers’ recommendations. With regards to the 
electricity meter, as mentioned above, it is a high accuracy measurement device/s and meets all relevant 
metrological requirements prescribed by the state authority. Procedures for maintenance of the meter 
will be conducted in accordance with national procedures and standards.  
Applied methodology ACM0022 requires sampling for the baseline emissions calculation. Sampling takes 
place at the inlet of the feeding system to the reac tors (mechanical conveyor system), to ensure the 
collected material is representative of the screened organic fraction. Each sampling event will include at 
least three grab samples taken at different times du ring the day (morning, midday, evening), with each 
grab sample weighing at least 10 kg, leading to a total sample weight of at least 200 kg per event. 
Collected samples are homogenized and reduced using standard procedures such as the quartering 
method or coning & splitting to obtain a 5–10 kg laboratory sample. Each type of waste is separated and 
measured separately, to calculate their composition. 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
128 
As required by the applied methodology ACM0022 and in accordance with the CDM methodological tool 
“Emissions from solid waste disposal sites” (TOOL 04, v8.1) for Application B, a sampling plan is 
implemented to quantify the composition of the organic fraction of municipal solid waste that would 
otherwise be disposed of at a solid waste disposal site. 
Sampling is conducted at the inlet of the feeding sy stem to the anaerobic digestion reactors (mechanical 
conveyor system), which represent the screened organic fraction. Sampling events are conducted 
throughout the crediting period, in alignmen t with TOOL04 requirements for parameters ρ
n,j,y (waste 
fraction in sample n) and z x (number of samples collected in year x). 
Each sampling event includes at least three grab sa mples collected at different times during the day, 
specifically in the morning, at midday, and in the evening. Each grab sample weighs a minimum of 10 
kilograms, leading to a total composite sample weight  of at least 200 kilogram s per event. The collected 
material is homogenized and reduced using standard procedures, such as the quartering method or 
coning and splitting, to yield a 5–10 kilogram representative sample for laboratory analysis. 
In the laboratory, the sample is segregated by waste type in accordance with TOOL04’s default categories 
for degradable organic carbon (DOC
j) and decay rates (k j), such as food waste, paper, garden waste, and 
inert materials. The weight of each waste type is measured on a wet basis to determine its weight fraction 
within the sample. These weight fractions ( ρn,j,y) are recorded and used to calculate average yearly waste 
compositions ( ρj,x), and the number of valid samples collect ed during the year is recorded as z x. 
This sampling procedure ensures compliance with th e TOOL04 requirement of collecting at least three 
samples every three months. By conducting three sampling campaign every three month, the project 
achieves at least 12 valid samples per year, generating consistent and representative waste composition 
data throughout the crediting period. 
Planning and Scheduling Internal Audits 
The quality assurance manager (QAM) shall develop an annual internal audit schedule covering all 
relevant processes, including waste sorting, anaerobi c digestion, landfill operations, biogas production, 
electricity generation, and associated measurement devi ces. The schedule shall provide that all critical 
processes are audited at least once per year. Auditors assigned shall be independent of the activities 
they audit to maintain objectivity. 
Conducting Internal Audits 
Auditors shall prepare an audit plan outlining the scope, objectives, criteria, and methods. The audit shall 
include: Review of process documentation and records (e.g., weighbridge records, waste characterization 
data, meter readings); interviews with personnel invo lved in the processes, and On-site observations of 
operations and equipment (e.g., biogas analyzer, flowmeters). Audit findings shall be based on objective 
evidence collected during the audit 
Identifying non-conformities 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
129 
Non-conformities shall be identified when deviat ions from procedures, standards, or regulatory 
requirements are observed. Each non-conformity shall be documented with: a clear description of the 
issue, reference to the specific requirement or st andard violated, and evidence supporting the finding 
(e.g., photographs, records.  
Reporting audit findings 
Auditors shall compile an internal audit report su mmarizing: audit scope and objectives, areas audited, 
non-conformities identified, and ob servations and opportunities for improvement. The report shall be 
submitted to the QAM and relevant department managers within five working days of the audit completion 
Addressing non-conformities 
Department managers shall review the non-conformities  relevant to their areas. A root cause analysis 
shall be performed for each non-conformity to identify underlying issues. A corrective action plan (CAP) 
shall be developed, including: Actions to address immediate issues; Steps to eliminate root causes; and 
responsibilities and timelines. The CAP shall be submitted to the QAM for approval within ten working 
days of receiving the audit report. 
Implementing Corrective Actions 
Department managers shall ensure corrective ac tions are implemented as per the approved CAP. 
Progress shall be monitored and do cumented. Any delays or obstacles shall be communicated to the 
QAM promptly 
Verification and Closure 
Upon completion of corrective actions, the QAM or de signated auditor shall verify  the effectiveness of the 
actions taken. verification must include one of th e items: re-auditing the affected areas; Reviewing 
updated records and document ation; and confirming changes with personnel. If the corrective action is 
effective, the non-conformity shall be closed, and records updated. Ineffective corrective actions shall 
require a reassessment and develo pment of additional actions. 
Documentation and Records 
All audit reports, non-conformity records, CAPs, and verification records shall be maintained by the QAM. 
Records shall be retained for a minimum of five  years and be readily accessible for review. 
Continuous Improvement 
QAM shall analyze audit results periodically to identify trends and systemic issues. Findings shall be 
communicated to senior management for strategic de cision-making. Lessons lear ned shall be integrated 
into training programs and procedure updates 
 
Calibration plans:  
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
130 
Calibration of the necessary equipment will be done according to the manufacturer’ guidelines. 
Flowmeters are usually calibrated every 5 years howeve r this period is dependent on the site conditions. 
A third party (either manufacturer or accredited laboratories) facility will be used for flowmeter calibration 
service (as of December 2023, factor y calibration is still valid). Flowmeters are located outside, mounted 
in pipeline. Measurement range is -10 to +10 mbar with a 0.035% reference accuracy. 
The gas engines have their own monitoring system and parameters can be seen every time on the display. 
There is an internal storage of the data on PC and on the cloud. All data can be downloaded in an excel 
file. 
Gas Analyzers are calibrated by the manufacturer’s te chnicians (MRU BACA GAZI LTD) on-site and ideally 
they should be calibrated every 6 months. However, due to on-site conditions these periods can be 
changed (based on observation). Th e gas analyzer is located indoors, within booster unit container. 
Methane and carbon dioxide content is measured with a 0 – 100% range and 0.01% (v) accuracy. Oxygen 
content is measured with a 0 – 25% range and 0.01% (v) accuracy. Hydrogen sulfide content is measured 
with a 0 – 5000 ppm range and 1 ppm accuracy 
Electricity meters are calibrated and owned by the government agency TEIAS, and the estimated 
calibration period is 10 years. Usual practice is government replacing old meters with calibrated new 
meters on site. Meters are located indoors. Metering accuracy is class C (0.5)/1.0. It must be noted that 
meters are sealed and can only be replaced/ calibrated by official government agencies. It has ben noted 
that electricity meters are replaced well before 10 years time when difference between main and 
secondary electricity meter exceeds certain limits. 
Weighbridge is calibration period is biannual. The Weighbridge operation is under the jurisdiction of 
project proponent

text_blocks.heading: 86 and calibrate the weighbridge every two years.
text_blocks.text:
Emergencies Procedures: 
The running hours of the power plants will be monitored as part of the monitoring procedures. In case of 
failure of one of the monitoring devices, portable inst ruments will be used in order to carry out periodic 
d a i l y  m o n i t o r i n g  o f  t h e  m i s s i n g  p a r a m e t e r ( s ) .  T h e s e  d a t a  a r e  r e c o r d e d  o n  p a p e r .  B i o t r e n d  d e f i n e d  
emergency procedures according to the provider recommendations. General procedure is as follow:  
Detection and Reporting: Upon detecting a meter failure or abnormal readings, the employee must 
immediately notify the Shift Supervisor. If the failur e poses an immediate safety risk (e.g., gas leaks), 
activate the emergency shutdown procedures and alarm systems. 
Initial Assessment: The Shift Supervisor shall assess the situation to determine the extent of the failure 
and potential impacts on safety and operations. If ne cessary, isolate the affected area or equipment to 
prevent further risks.

text_blocks.heading: 86 Some municipalities operate weighbridge themselves.
text_blocks.text:
Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
131 
Notification: The Shift Supervisor must inform the Operations Manager and Maintenance Manager 
immediately. If regulatory reporting is required (e.g., environmental release), the Operations Manager will 
handle communications with authorities. 
Implementation of Contingency Measures: 
Activation of Backup Systems: If available, switch to  backup meters or systems to maintain monitoring 
capabilities. Verify the functionality and calibration of backup devices before use. 
Manual Monitoring: Implement manual data collection procedures using portable instruments. Assign 
qualified personnel to record readings at specified intervals. 
Operational Adjustments: Adjust operational parameters  to safe levels based on available data. Reduce 
or suspend operations if safety or compliance cannot be assured. 
Repair and Restoration 
Fault Diagnosis: The maintenance manager shall assign  technicians to diagnose the cause of the meter 
failure. Utilize diagnostic tools and consult equipment manuals as needed. 
Repair or Replacement: Repair the failed meter if fe asible; otherwise, replace it  with a calibrated unit. 
Ensure all repairs or replacements meet manufacturer specifications and regulatory requirements. 
Calibration and Testing: Calibrate the repaired or new meter accordin g to the Calibration Procedure. 
Conduct functional tests to confirm accurate operation. 
Communication and Documentation 
Internal Communication: Provide regular updates to all relevant departments during the emergency. 
Debrief all staff once normal  operations are restored. 
External Communication: If required, the Operations Manager will report the incident to regulatory bodies 
within the stipulated timeframe. Prepare inci dent reports as per regulatory guidelines. 
Record Keeping: Document all actions taken during the emergency, including timelines, personnel 
involved, and decisions made. File all records in accordance with the Document Control Procedure. 
Post-Emergency Review 
Incident Investigation: Conduct a root cause analysis  to identify the underlying causes of the meter 
failure. Use appropriate methodologies (e.g., 5 Whys, Fault Tree Analysis). 
Corrective Actions: Develop a Corrective Action Plan to address identified issues. Assign responsibilities 
and set deadlines for implementation. 
Preventive Measures: Update maintenance schedules to  prevent future occurren ces. Review and revise 
operational procedures if necessary. 
Training: Conduct training sessions for staff on new procedures or changes. Ensure all training is 
documented and filed appropriately. 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4.2 
132 
Safety Considerations 
Always prioritize personal and environmental safety over operational concerns. Use appropriate Personal 
Protective Equipment (PPE) during all emergency and repair activities. Follow all lockout/tagout 
procedures when working on electrical or mechanical systems. 
Documentation 
Maintain records of the following documents: Incident Reports, Repair and Maintenance Logs, Calibration 
Certificates, Communication Logs, Training Records, Root Cause Analysis Reports and Corrective and 
Preventive Action Plans.
